# P3 — PROACTIVE PATH AGREEMENT TEMPLATE
## Supporting Process | Client-Facing Contract

```
SOP Name:     P3 — Proactive Path Agreement Template
Work Type:    Predefined — sent to every new PP client at enrollment
Who owns it:  Owner / CEO (Marcin)
How often:    Once per new PP client
Last updated: 2026-05-28

NOTE: This is a template. Have it reviewed by a WA-licensed attorney before
use with paying clients. This document does not constitute legal advice.
```

---

## PROACTIVE PATH AGREEMENT
### Handy Pioneers LLC

---

**Client Name:** ___________________________
**Property Address:** ___________________________
**Agreement Date:** ___________________________
**Program Tier:** ☐ Essential ($59/mo) ☐ Full Coverage ($99/mo) ☐ Maximum Protection ($149/mo)
**Billing Cadence:** ☐ Monthly ☐ Quarterly ☐ Annual
**Founding Member Rate (if applicable):** ___________________________
**Annual Renewal Date:** ___________________________

---

### 1. WHAT HANDY PIONEERS WILL DELIVER

Handy Pioneers LLC ("HP") will deliver the following services under the Proactive Path program:

**All Tiers — Included in Every Membership:**
- One (1) annual 360° Home Scan (Baseline Assessment for new clients; recurring annually)
- Prioritized findings report with written scope delivered within 5 business days
- Client portal access — all visit records, photos, and findings documented
- Member rates on all out-of-scope work (discount tier per membership level)
- HP direct line — single point of contact for all home maintenance coordination

**Essential — 2 Seasonal Visits:**
- Spring visit + Fall visit
- Member discount: 5% (<$1k) / 3% ($1k–$5k) / 1.5% (>$5k) on out-of-scope work

**Full Coverage — 4 Seasonal Visits + Labor Bank:**
- All four seasonal visits (Spring, Summer, Fall, Winter)
- $300 annual labor bank credit applied to HP self-performed roadmap work
- Summer prep/weatherization and winter freeze/moisture visit included
- Annual equity documentation report
- Member discount: 8% (<$1k) / 5% ($1k–$5k) / 2.5% (>$5k) on out-of-scope work
- Labor bank credit applies to HP self-performed work; trade costs billed separately

**Maximum Protection — 4 Seasonal Visits + Priority Access + Full Labor Bank:**
- All four seasonal visits (Spring, Summer, Fall, Winter)
- $600 annual labor bank credit applied to HP self-performed roadmap work
- Priority scheduling (within 5 business days for non-emergency requests)
- Dedicated HP account manager
- Pre-negotiated vetted-tradesman rates on major work
- Home equity maintenance log for refinancing/sale documentation
- Member discount: 12% (<$1k) / 8% ($1k–$5k) / 4% (>$5k) on out-of-scope work

---

### 2. WHAT IS NOT INCLUDED

The following are not included in the subscription and will be quoted separately:
- Materials for any repair or project work
- Subcontractor (trade) costs — plumbing, electrical, HVAC, roofing
- Any project work beyond the Full Coverage ($300) or Maximum Protection ($600) labor bank credit
- Emergency after-hours response (available at standard billing rates)
- Permit fees required for any permitted scope

---

### 3. PAYMENT TERMS

- Monthly subscription fee due on the same date each month
- First payment due at agreement signing
- Billing cadences: monthly / quarterly / annual (annual rate locked at signing)
- Annual prepay reflects the annual price listed above (already discounted vs. monthly)
- Late payment (>10 days): HP may pause service until account is current
- Returned payment fee: $35

---

### 4. PROJECT WORK BILLING

All project work outside the subscription scope will be quoted in writing before any work begins. HP's standard billing applies:
- Written scope provided before every estimate
- Client written approval required before any work starts
- Payment terms: 50% deposit at scheduling / 50% at completion for jobs over $2,000
- Jobs under $2,000: payment due at completion unless otherwise agreed in writing

---

### 5. LABOR GUARANTEE

HP provides a 1-year labor guarantee on all self-performed work. If a defect in HP's workmanship is identified within 12 months of completion, HP will correct it at no charge. This guarantee does not cover:
- Material failure or manufacturer defects
- Client-directed scope changes
- Damage caused by subsequent work by others
- Normal wear and deterioration

---

### 6. SCHEDULING

HP will schedule and confirm all seasonal visits in advance. Client agrees to:
- Provide reasonable access to the property for scheduled visits
- Provide 48-hour notice if rescheduling is needed
- Allow HP to reschedule within the same season if HP requests a change

---

### 7. CANCELLATION

**Client cancellation:** Client may cancel with 30 days written notice. No refund of the current month's payment. Any outstanding project invoices remain due.

**HP cancellation:** HP may cancel this agreement with 30 days written notice if the client repeatedly fails to provide access, or if payment is more than 30 days past due.

---

### 8. LIMITATION OF LIABILITY

HP's liability under this agreement is limited to the value of services provided in the prior 12 months. HP is not liable for pre-existing conditions, latent defects not discoverable through visual inspection, or consequential damages.

---

### 9. GOVERNING LAW

This agreement is governed by the laws of the State of Washington. Any disputes will be resolved in Clark County, WA.

---

### SIGNATURES

**Client:**

Signature: ___________________________ Date: ___________
Print Name: ___________________________

**Handy Pioneers LLC:**

Signature: ___________________________ Date: ___________
Print Name: Marcin Micek, Owner
WA Lic. HANDYP★761NH | $1M General Liability Insurance

---

*Questions: (360) 838-6731 | help@handypioneers.com | handypioneers.com*

docs.body:
# P4 — ESTIMATING & MARGIN AUDIT SOP
## Supporting Process | INTERNAL DOCUMENT — CONFIDENTIAL

```
SOP Name:     P4 — Estimating & Margin Audit SOP
Work Type:    Predefined — runs before every customer-facing estimate
Who owns it:  Office / Estimator (Owner until Office hired)
How often:    Every job estimate before it is sent to a client
Last updated: 2026-05-28

WARNING: This document contains internal cost rates, markup math, and margin rules.
NEVER share this document or its contents with any client, prospect, or subcontractor.
```

---

## OBJECTIVE
Every estimate sent to a client has passed the margin audit. No estimate leaves HP without confirming it meets the gross margin floor for its job size.

---

## HP MARGIN RULES (INTERNAL)

```
Rule                              Threshold                  Requirement
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Gross Margin Floor (Standard)     All jobs                   Minimum 30% GM
Gross Margin Floor (Small Job)    Hard cost under $2,000     Minimum 40% GM
Internal Labor Cost Rate          HP technicians             $100/hr [INTERNAL]
Internal Labor Billing Rate       All clients                $150/hr [INTERNAL]
Subcontractor Markup              All sub quotes             Per tier table below
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```

---

## SUBCONTRACTOR MARKUP TIERS

```
Job Size (Hard Cost)    Min Markup    Target Markup    Notes
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Under $500              67%           80%+             40% GM floor. Minimum job fee may apply.
$500 – $2,000           67%           75%              40% GM floor. Use higher end of range.
$2,001 – $5,000         43%           50%              Standard 30% GM. Target 33%+.
$5,001 – $15,000        43%           48%              Standard. Larger jobs may need comp check.
Over $15,000            40%           45%              Competitive range. Never below 30% GM.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Sub #1 (Carpenter): $100/hr → bills at $150/hr = 33% GM on labor only.
  ⚠ FAILS the 40% small job floor.
  Do NOT use Sub #1 on jobs with hard cost under $2,000 without raising the billing rate.
  Minimum billing rate for Sub #1 on small jobs: $167/hr to hit 40% GM floor.
```

---

## GM CALCULATION FORMULA

```
Gross Margin % = (Revenue − Hard Cost) / Revenue

Hard Cost = labor (internal + sub) + materials + trade quotes

Examples:
  Hard Cost $1,200 → Minimum price at 40% GM:
    Revenue = $1,200 / 0.60 = $2,000 minimum
    (Hard cost under $2K — 40% GM floor applies)

  Hard Cost $4,000 → Minimum price at 30% GM:
    Revenue = $4,000 / 0.70 = $5,714 minimum
    (Hard cost over $2K — standard 30% GM floor)

  8 hrs HP tech + $800 materials:
    Cost = $800 + (8 × $100) = $1,600 total hard cost
    Under $2K → 40% GM applies
    Revenue = $1,600 / 0.60 = $2,667 minimum
```

---

## PRE-SEND AUDIT CHECKLIST

Run before every estimate is sent to a client:

```
[ ] Hard cost totaled correctly (labor + materials + all sub quotes)
[ ] Correct GM tier applied: under $2K → 40%, over $2K → 30%
[ ] NO internal rates visible anywhere in the estimate
[ ] NO markup math visible anywhere in the estimate
[ ] Customer-facing language only in estimate body
[ ] Payment terms included (50% deposit / 50% completion for jobs over $2K)
[ ] Scope clearly states what IS and IS NOT included
[ ] Estimate valid period noted (30 days standard)
```

---

## CUSTOMER-FACING ESTIMATE RULES

**Format:** Plain text — scope bullets + total investment + payment terms.

**Never include:**
- Markup %
- Cost line items
- Subcontractor names or rates
- Internal labor rates
- Any math showing how the price was calculated

**Always include:**
- What's included
- What's excluded
- How long it takes
- Who does the work (HP or HP-coordinated trade)
- Payment schedule

---

## ESTIMATE OUTPUT TEMPLATE

```
ESTIMATE — [Client Name]  |  [Date]
Project: [Brief title — e.g., "Deck Board Replacement"]

Scope of Work:
  • [Bullet 1 — what will be done]
  • [Bullet 2 — what will be done]
  • [Bullet 3 — what will NOT be done, if relevant]

Total Investment: $X,XXX
Deposit (due at scheduling): 50% — $X,XXX
Balance (due at completion): 50% — $X,XXX

Estimated Duration: [X days / X hours]
This estimate is valid for 30 days.

Handy Pioneers LLC | WA Lic. HANDYP★761NH | 1-Year Labor Guarantee
(360) 838-6731 | handypioneers.com
```

---

## MARGIN FLAG PROTOCOL

```
Any job finishing BELOW margin floor → IDS trigger, same day.
  - Do not wait until month-end.
  - Flag to Marcin before invoice goes out.
  - Document the variance and root cause in job file.

IDS trigger conditions:
  - Any job finishing below 30% GM (standard) or 40% GM (small job)
  - Any estimate variance exceeding 15% of final cost
  - Any scope change that was not re-estimated and approved in writing
```

---

## RELATED SOPs
- S4 — Prioritize (ROM ranges in roadmap must be consistent with margin floors)
- S5 — Schedule (no project scheduled without signed estimate)
- S6 — Execute (margin audit runs before invoicing every completed job)
docs.title: P4 — ESTIMATING & MARGIN AUDIT SOP

docs.body:
# P5 — INVOICE & COLLECTIONS SOP
## Supporting Process | Cash Flow From Job One

```
SOP Name:     P5 — Invoice & Collections SOP
Work Type:    Predefined — runs after every completed job and on monthly subscription billing
Who owns it:  Office / Estimator (Owner until Office hired)
How often:    Per job completion + monthly for PP subscriptions
Last updated: 2026-05-28
```

---

## OBJECTIVE
Every completed job is invoiced promptly. Every invoice is collected. Open receivables never age past 30 days. Cash flow is not a bottleneck.

---

## INVOICE TYPES

```
Type 1 — PP Subscription (recurring)
  Amount: Per tier and cadence:
    Essential:          $59/mo  | $169/quarter | $588/annual
    Full Coverage:      $99/mo  | $279/quarter | $948/annual
    Maximum Protection: $149/mo | $419/quarter | $1,428/annual
  Due:    Per cadence selected at enrollment (monthly = same date each month)
  Method: [HP Estimator app / preferred payment processor]

Type 2 — Project Job (one-off or roadmap execution)
  Trigger: Job completion + client sign-off documented in portal (S6)
  Structure:
    Jobs over $2,000:  50% deposit invoiced at scheduling; 50% balance at completion
    Jobs under $2,000: 100% due at completion (or deposit by agreement)

Type 3 — Labor Bank Credit Reconciliation (Full Coverage + Maximum Protection only)
  Full Coverage: Track labor credit used vs. $300 annual bank
  Maximum Protection: Track labor credit used vs. $600 annual bank
  When credit is exhausted: bill additional labor at standard member rates
  Client must be notified before credit is exhausted — no surprise invoices
```

---

## PROJECT INVOICE PROTOCOL

```
Step 1:  Confirm job is complete — client sign-off in portal (S6 Execute SOP)
Step 2:  Run margin check — final hard cost vs. estimate (P4 Estimating SOP)
           If final cost exceeds estimate → flag to Marcin before invoicing
Step 3:  Build invoice in [HP Estimator app]
           Include: job description, scope completed, line item total, payment terms
           Do NOT include: cost math, markup, internal rates
Step 4:  Attach signed estimate reference to invoice
Step 5:  Send invoice within 24 hours of job completion
Step 6:  Log invoice in HP records: client, date, amount, due date
Step 7:  Attach invoice reference to portal roadmap item (S3 Tracking SOP)
```

---

## INVOICE FORMAT — CLIENT-FACING

```
INVOICE — [Client Name]
Date: [Invoice Date]
Invoice #: [HP-YYYY-###]
Due Date: [Date — net 7 or net 15 per agreement]

Description: [Job title — e.g., "Deck Board Replacement — Spring 2026"]
Scope completed: [1–3 bullet summary matching the estimate scope]

Amount Due: $X,XXX

Payment options: [methods accepted]

Handy Pioneers LLC | WA Lic. HANDYP★761NH
(360) 838-6731 | help@handypioneers.com
```

---

## COLLECTIONS PROTOCOL

```
Day 0 (due date):   Invoice due. Confirm payment method is active.

Day 3 (no payment): Send friendly reminder — "Just checking in on invoice #HP-XXX."
                    Email or text — client's preferred contact method.

Day 10:             Second reminder. Phone call if no response to prior contact.
                    "Wanted to make sure the invoice came through clearly."

Day 20:             Direct conversation — Marcin contacts client personally.
                    Do not let this reach 30 days without owner involvement.

Day 30:             Escalation. Pause any scheduled work until account is current.
                    Document in client file. IDS trigger if PP client.

Over 30 days:       Formal written notice. Review PP agreement cancellation clause.
                    Flag to Marcin for decision on relationship continuation.
```

---

## OPEN INVOICE TARGET

```
Open invoices > 30 days: $0 always
If any invoice reaches 30 days unpaid: IDS trigger at next L10
Monthly review: check all open invoices — no invoice should age unnoticed
```

---

## SUB PAYMENT PROTOCOL

```
All subcontractors: Net-7 after HP receives client payment
  Do NOT pay subs before client payment is collected
  Sub invoice received → hold until client payment clears → pay sub within 7 days
  Log sub payments separately from client invoices
```

---

## DOCUMENTATION REQUIRED
```
[ ] Invoice sent within 24 hours of job completion
[ ] Invoice reference logged in portal against the roadmap item
[ ] Final margin confirmed before invoicing (P4 check)
[ ] Collections contacts documented if payment is late
[ ] Sub payment logged after client payment received
```

---

## RELATED SOPs
- S6 — Execute (triggers this SOP at job completion)
- P4 — Estimating & Margin Audit (margin check before every invoice)
- P7 — Sub Agreement Template (sub payment terms governed there)
docs.title: P5 — INVOICE & COLLECTIONS SOP

docs.body:
# P6 — JOB COSTING WORKSHEET
## Supporting Process | INTERNAL DOCUMENT — CONFIDENTIAL

```
SOP Name:     P6 — Job Costing Worksheet
Work Type:    Predefined — completed after every project job
Who owns it:  Owner / CEO (Marcin)
How often:    Per job — post-completion review
Last updated: 2026-05-28
```

---

## OBJECTIVE
Know the actual margin on every job — not just the estimated margin. Compare estimated vs. actual cost on every project to catch variance, improve estimating accuracy, and protect margin over time.

---

## JOB COSTING WORKSHEET

Complete after every project job is invoiced and closed.

```
JOB COSTING — HANDY PIONEERS                    [INTERNAL — CONFIDENTIAL]
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Job:              [Job title / roadmap item]
Client:           [Client name]
Invoice #:        [HP-YYYY-###]
Job Date:         [Date completed]
Job Type:         HP Self-Performed / Sub-Led / Mixed
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

REVENUE
  Estimated revenue:    $________
  Actual revenue:       $________
  Variance:             $________   [ ] Within 15%  [ ] FLAG — over 15%

HARD COSTS
  Labor — HP (actual hours × $100/hr):
    Estimated:    ____hrs × $100 = $________
    Actual:       ____hrs × $100 = $________
    Variance:     $________

  Labor — Sub (actual invoice):
    Estimated:    $________
    Actual:       $________
    Variance:     $________
    ⚠ Sub #1 (Carpenter) check: If hard cost < $2,000, was 40% GM floor met?

  Materials (actual receipts):
    Estimated:    $________
    Actual:       $________
    Variance:     $________

  Permit fees (if applicable):
    Estimated:    $________
    Actual:       $________

  TOTAL HARD COST:
    Estimated:    $________
    Actual:       $________
    Variance:     $________   [ ] Within 15%  [ ] FLAG — over 15%

GROSS MARGIN
  Estimated GM:   (Est. Revenue − Est. Hard Cost) / Est. Revenue = _____%
  Actual GM:      (Act. Revenue − Act. Hard Cost) / Act. Revenue = _____%
  GM Floor:       [ ] ≥30% (standard)  [ ] ≥40% (small job, hard cost <$2K)
  Result:         [ ] PASS  [ ] FAIL — IDS trigger required

VARIANCE ANALYSIS (complete if any variance > 15%)
  What caused the variance?
  _______________________________________________________________
  Was a scope change involved?  [ ] Yes — was it approved in writing?  [ ] No
  Was this an estimating error?  [ ] Yes — adjust future estimates how?
  _______________________________________________________________

LESSONS LEARNED
  What would you do differently on the next similar job?
  _______________________________________________________________
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```

---

## IDS TRIGGERS FROM JOB COSTING

```
Trigger IDS at the next L10 if:
  [ ] Actual GM falls below the floor (30% standard / 40% small job)
  [ ] Any cost variance exceeds 15% of the estimate
  [ ] Revenue variance exceeds 15% (scope change that wasn't re-estimated)
  [ ] Same variance type appears on 2+ consecutive jobs (systemic estimating issue)
```

---

## MONTHLY ROLL-UP

At the end of each month, summarize all job costing sheets:

```
Month:            ___________
Jobs completed:   ___
Total revenue:    $________
Total hard cost:  $________
Blended GM:       _____%   Target: 35%+
Jobs below floor: ___       Target: 0
IDS items raised: ___
```

Feed the blended GM into P16 (Monthly P&L Review).

---

## RELATED SOPs
- P4 — Estimating & Margin Audit (estimated costs come from here)
- P5 — Invoice & Collections (invoice must be sent before job costing is complete)
- P16 — Monthly P&L Review (uses job costing roll-up)
- S6 — Execute (triggers job costing at completion)

# P7 — SUB AGREEMENT TEMPLATE
## Supporting Process | Subcontractor Onboarding & Terms

```
SOP Name:     P7 — Sub Agreement Template
Work Type:    Predefined — completed before using any sub on an HP job
Who owns it:  Owner / CEO (Marcin)
How often:    Once per subcontractor relationship (renewed annually)
Last updated: 2026-05-28

NOTE: Have this reviewed by a WA-licensed attorney before use.
```

---

## SUB ONBOARDING CHECKLIST

Before any sub works on an HP job for the first time:

```
[ ] Sub Agreement signed (this document)
[ ] Certificate of Insurance (COI) on file — $1M general liability minimum
[ ] WA contractor license verified (if applicable to trade)
[ ] Contact info, payment method, and preferred scheduling method confirmed
[ ] HP brand conduct expectations reviewed and acknowledged
[ ] Sub understands: all client communication routes through HP only
```

---

## SUBCONTRACTOR AGREEMENT
### Handy Pioneers LLC

---

**Subcontractor Name / Business:** ___________________________
**License # (if applicable):** ___________________________
**Phone:** ___________________________
**Email:** ___________________________
**Trade / Specialty:** ___________________________
**Agreement Date:** ___________________________

---

### 1. RELATIONSHIP

This agreement establishes the terms under which [Sub Name] ("Subcontractor") will perform work on behalf of Handy Pioneers LLC ("HP") for HP's clients. Subcontractor is an independent contractor, not an employee of HP.

---

### 2. SCOPE OF WORK

Scope for each job will be provided in writing by HP before any work begins. Subcontractor agrees to:
- Review and accept the written scope before starting any job
- Notify HP immediately if the actual scope differs from what was described
- Not expand scope without written HP authorization
- Not perform work outside the agreed scope on HP client properties

---

### 3. CLIENT CONDUCT

Subcontractor agrees to the following when on HP client property:
- Present professionally — clean appearance, no profanity, no aggressive behavior
- Do not discuss pricing, HP's business, or other clients with the client
- All client communication routes through HP — Subcontractor does not call, text, or email HP clients directly except for on-site coordination
- Do not solicit HP clients for direct work outside of HP
- Leave the site clean — no tools, debris, or materials left behind
- Take before/after photos of all work completed and send to HP same day

---

### 4. PAYMENT TERMS

- HP will pay Subcontractor within 7 business days of HP receiving client payment
- HP does not pay subs before client payment is collected
- Subcontractor invoices HP — not the client
- Invoice must reference the HP job number
- Disputed invoices: HP will notify Subcontractor within 3 business days of receipt

---

### 5. INSURANCE

Subcontractor must maintain at minimum:
- $1,000,000 General Liability Insurance
- Workers Compensation if Subcontractor has employees
- COI naming Handy Pioneers LLC as additional insured must be on file before first job

---

### 6. QUALITY STANDARD

Subcontractor agrees to perform all work to HP's quality standard:
- All work to current WA building code
- Materials to be new and of standard quality unless otherwise specified
- No shortcuts that reduce the service life of completed work

**Performance review trigger:**
- 2 quality callbacks on HP jobs: mandatory review meeting with Marcin
- 1 no-show on a confirmed HP job: mandatory review meeting with Marcin
- Either condition may result in suspension from HP's sub roster

---

### 7. NON-SOLICITATION

For 12 months following the last HP job, Subcontractor agrees not to directly solicit or accept direct work from HP clients they were introduced to through HP. This does not apply to clients the Subcontractor had a prior relationship with before being introduced through HP.

---

### 8. TERMINATION

Either party may terminate this agreement with 7 days written notice. Termination does not affect obligations for jobs already in progress.

---

### SIGNATURES

**Subcontractor:**

Signature: ___________________________ Date: ___________
Print Name: ___________________________
Business Name: ___________________________

**Handy Pioneers LLC:**

Signature: ___________________________ Date: ___________
Print Name: Marcin Micek, Owner

---

## CURRENT SUB ROSTER STATUS

```
Sub #1 — Carpenter/Framer:    Agreement pending  COI pending
  Rate: $100/hr | ⚠ Fails 40% GM small job floor — do not use on hard cost <$2K
Sub #2 — Plumber:             Agreement pending  COI pending
Sub #3 — Electrician:         Agreement pending  COI pending  ⚠ Reliability concern
Sub #4 — Drywall/Paint:       Agreement pending  COI pending
Sub GAP — HVAC:               Not yet sourced — required before first PP seasonal cycle
```

# P8 — SUB PERFORMANCE SCORECARD
## Supporting Process | Sub Roster Quality Control

```
SOP Name:     P8 — Sub Performance Scorecard
Work Type:    Predefined — updated after every sub-led or sub-assisted job
Who owns it:  Lead Technician (Owner until Lead Tech hired)
How often:    After every job involving a sub. Reviewed quarterly.
Last updated: 2026-05-28
```

---

## OBJECTIVE
Track sub performance across every HP job so roster decisions are based on documented history, not memory. Know which subs to call first — and when to replace one.

---

## SCORECARD — PER SUB

Maintain one scorecard per sub. Update after every job.

```
SUBCONTRACTOR SCORECARD — HANDY PIONEERS        [INTERNAL]
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Sub Name / Trade:     ___________________________
Agreement on file:    [ ] Yes  [ ] No — do not use until signed
COI current:          [ ] Yes  [ ] No — do not use until on file
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

JOB LOG
Date       Job / Client      On Time  Quality  Client Conduct  Invoice Accurate  Notes
━━━━━━━━   ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━   ━━━━━━━  ━━━━━━━  ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  ━━━━━━
           |                 Y/N      1–5      Y/N             Y/N
           |                 Y/N      1–5      Y/N             Y/N
           |                 Y/N      1–5      Y/N             Y/N
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

QUALITY RATING SCALE
  5 — Excellent: exceeded scope expectations, client compliment
  4 — Good: met scope, clean work, no issues
  3 — Acceptable: completed scope, minor concerns noted
  2 — Below standard: required follow-up or correction
  1 — Unacceptable: callback, redo, or client complaint

AUTOMATIC REVIEW TRIGGERS (log immediately when either occurs):
  [ ] 2 quality callbacks on HP jobs
  [ ] 1 confirmed no-show on a scheduled HP job

CURRENT STATUS:  Active / Under Review / Suspended / Replaced
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```

---

## QUARTERLY ROSTER REVIEW

Review all sub scorecards at the end of each quarter:

```
[ ] Any sub with avg quality < 3.5 over last 3 jobs: schedule review conversation
[ ] Any sub who triggered automatic review: resolve — continue or replace
[ ] Any sub with COI expired: do not schedule until renewed
[ ] HVAC gap: still not sourced? Escalate — needed before next PP seasonal cycle
[ ] Electrician reliability: still a concern? Source backup now
```

---

## REVIEW CONVERSATION PROTOCOL

If a sub triggers the automatic review:

```
Step 1:  Pull the job log — document the specific incidents
Step 2:  Direct conversation with Marcin — not a text
Step 3:  State what happened, what the standard is, what needs to change
Step 4:  Sub either confirms they understand and commits — or they're off the roster
Step 5:  Log the conversation in the scorecard with date and outcome
```

---

## RELATED SOPs
- P7 — Sub Agreement Template (agreement must be on file before any job)
- S6 — Execute (sub quality is verified at job sign-off)
- S7 — Preservation Projects / S8 — Strategic Upgrades (sub quality critical for Phase 3)

docs.body:
# P9 — SEASONAL VISIT CHECKLISTS (4x)
## Supporting Process | Field Reference Sheets — Clark County, WA (PNW)

```
SOP Name:     P9 — Seasonal Visit Checklists
Work Type:    Predefined — field reference for every seasonal visit
Who owns it:  Lead Technician (Owner until Lead Tech hired)
How often:    4x per year per client — Spring / Summer / Fall / Winter
Last updated: 2026-05-28

Used by:      S2 (Seasonal Inspection Log) — the "what to check" list
              S5 (Schedule) — seasonal visit scope reference
```

---

> These are standalone field checklists. Bring the relevant sheet to every visit.
> Check every item. Mark: ✓ (Good) / FLAG (Needs Attention or worse) / N/A
> Any FLAG → document in portal (S3) before leaving the property.

---

## SPRING CHECKLIST
### Target: March – April | Post-winter inspection + gutter clearing

**Roof & Gutters**
```
[ ] Walk the roofline — look for missing, curled, or cracked shingles
[ ] Check for moss or lichen growth (endemic in Clark County — treat if present)
[ ] Inspect flashing at chimney, vents, skylights, and valleys
[ ] Clean gutters — remove all winter debris, flush with water
[ ] Check downspouts — clear blockage, confirm discharge away from foundation
[ ] Inspect fascia and soffit for rot or pest entry points
[ ] Note any ice dam evidence from winter (staining, insulation damage at eaves)
```

**Exterior Envelope**
```
[ ] Walk all four sides — inspect siding for cracking, warping, or moisture damage
[ ] Check all exterior caulking — windows, doors, penetrations, trim joints
[ ] Inspect window and door weatherstripping — replace if compressed or torn
[ ] Check exterior paint — note peeling, bubbling, or bare wood exposure
[ ] Inspect wood trim, fascia, and corner boards for rot (probe with screwdriver)
[ ] Check deck ledger board flashing (PNW moisture trap — common failure point)
```

**Foundation & Drainage**
```
[ ] Walk the perimeter — confirm grading slopes away from foundation (6" in 10 ft)
[ ] Check all downspout discharge points — not pooling near foundation
[ ] Inspect any visible foundation cracks — note size and location
[ ] Check crawl space vent screens — clear of debris and intact
[ ] If accessible: check crawl space for standing water or moisture after winter
```

**HVAC**
```
[ ] Replace HVAC filter — note size and install date in portal
[ ] Inspect exterior AC condenser unit — clear debris from winter, check fins
[ ] Check condensate drain line — clear and draining
[ ] Note thermostat — confirm client has switched to cooling mode
[ ] If heat pump: check refrigerant lines for insulation damage
```

**Plumbing**
```
[ ] Check hose bibs — confirm winterization was removed, no freeze damage
[ ] Run all exterior hose bibs — confirm flow and no drips at valve
[ ] Check water heater — note age, look for rust, drips, or corrosion at connections
[ ] Inspect pressure relief valve — no evidence of prior discharge
```

**Exterior Structures**
```
[ ] Inspect deck boards — rot, soft spots, loose fasteners, cracked boards
[ ] Check deck railings — structural integrity, no wobble
[ ] Inspect fence — posts for rot at base, boards for damage
[ ] Check driveway and walkways — winter frost heave cracks
[ ] Inspect any retaining walls — movement, drainage weep holes clear
```

**PNW Spring Flags**
```
[ ] Moss on roof — treat if >5% coverage or visibly spreading
[ ] Wood rot at any exterior penetration — probe every caulked joint
[ ] Crawl space moisture — spring is peak season for PNW moisture intrusion
[ ] Pest entry points — carpenter ants active in spring; check wood-to-soil contact
```

---

## SUMMER CHECKLIST
### Target: June – July | Exterior systems + HVAC performance

**Roof & Exterior**
```
[ ] Re-check any spring flag items — confirm no worsening
[ ] Inspect caulking at all window and door perimeters — summer heat reveals gaps
[ ] Check exterior paint for new blistering or peeling (heat accelerates failure)
[ ] Inspect any roof penetration boots — UV degradation is highest in summer
```

**HVAC**
```
[ ] Replace HVAC filter if 90 days since spring replacement
[ ] Test AC performance — confirm cooling to set temp within reasonable time
[ ] Check AC condenser coils — clean if dirty (hose down if needed)
[ ] Confirm condensate drain flowing — summer is peak condensate load
[ ] Check attic temperature — excessive heat may indicate ventilation issue
```

**Exterior Structures**
```
[ ] Inspect deck — boards for checking/cracking in dry heat
[ ] Check deck fasteners — summer expansion/contraction loosens screws
[ ] Inspect deck finish — seal or stain if water no longer beads
[ ] Check fence posts — ground movement from dry summer soil
[ ] Inspect any wood outbuildings or shed siding
```

**Plumbing**
```
[ ] Check all exterior hose bibs — usage season, confirm no slow leaks
[ ] Inspect irrigation system if present — heads, valves, controller settings
[ ] Check water heater anode rod if unit is 5+ years old (note for roadmap)
```

**Pest & Vegetation**
```
[ ] Check for vegetation contact with siding or roof — trim back 12" clearance
[ ] Inspect wood structures for carpenter ant or termite evidence
[ ] Check for wasp nests at eaves, soffits, or deck framing
[ ] Confirm no soil-to-wood contact at any exterior framing
```

**PNW Summer Flags**
```
[ ] Deck finish degradation — dry summer reveals previous-year weathering
[ ] Attic ventilation — PNW homes are often under-ventilated; check for heat trapping
[ ] Pest entry points — summer is active season for most wood-boring insects
```

---

## FALL CHECKLIST
### Target: September – October | Pre-winter prep + weatherization

**Roof & Gutters**
```
[ ] Clean gutters — primary fall service; leaf and debris removal before rain season
[ ] Flush gutters and downspouts — confirm clear before heavy rain begins
[ ] Check downspout extensions — confirm discharge route away from foundation
[ ] Inspect roof for any summer damage — loose shingles, lifted flashing
[ ] Apply moss treatment if growth visible (fall application most effective in PNW)
[ ] Check for any debris accumulation in roof valleys
```

**Exterior Weatherization**
```
[ ] Inspect and replace weatherstripping on all exterior doors
[ ] Check window and door caulking — re-caulk any gaps before cold weather
[ ] Inspect all exterior penetration caulking — dryer vents, hose bibs, pipes
[ ] Check exterior paint — note any bare wood that will absorb winter moisture
[ ] Confirm attic vents are clear and unobstructed before moisture season
```

**HVAC — Heating Prep**
```
[ ] Replace HVAC filter — clean filter critical for heating season efficiency
[ ] Test heat — switch to heat mode, confirm system reaches set temp
[ ] Inspect furnace or heat pump — note any unusual sounds or smells
[ ] Check all supply and return vents — clear of furniture and debris
[ ] If gas furnace: check pilot or igniter function; note for sub referral if uncertain
[ ] Schedule HVAC service if unit is 5+ years old and hasn't been serviced recently
```

**Plumbing — Winterization**
```
[ ] Winterize all exterior hose bibs — shut off interior valve, drain exterior
[ ] Label shut-off valves in utility area (if not already labeled)
[ ] Check for any dripping faucets — small drip = slow freeze risk
[ ] Check water heater insulation if in unconditioned space
[ ] Note any exposed pipes in garage, crawl space, or exterior walls (flag for roadmap)
```

**Foundation & Drainage**
```
[ ] Check grading around foundation — confirmed sloping away before rain season
[ ] Clear any debris from window wells
[ ] Inspect sump pump if present — test float, confirm discharge route clear
[ ] Check crawl space vents — confirm open for moisture exchange (PNW standard)
```

**Exterior Structures**
```
[ ] Apply deck sealant or stain if not done in summer — last window before rain
[ ] Check deck boards for new rot or soft spots after summer dry-out
[ ] Inspect fence posts — ground movement after summer; check for lean
[ ] Store or cover any seasonal outdoor furniture or equipment
```

**PNW Fall Flags**
```
[ ] Gutter clearing — #1 fall priority in Clark County; leaf season + heavy rain = overflow
[ ] Hose bib winterization — freeze events can arrive suddenly in November
[ ] Crawl space moisture prep — confirm no standing water from prior season
[ ] Roof moss treatment — fall application prevents spring overgrowth
```

---

## WINTER CHECKLIST
### Target: December – January | Interior systems + heating + moisture monitoring

**Heating Systems**
```
[ ] Replace HVAC filter if 90 days since fall replacement
[ ] Confirm thermostat is functioning correctly and client is comfortable with settings
[ ] Check all baseboard heaters if present — clear of obstructions
[ ] Check fireplace if present — flue open/clear, damper functioning (note sub referral
    for chimney inspection if not done in past 2 years)
[ ] Inspect weatherstripping on all exterior doors — cold air infiltration check
```

**Attic**
```
[ ] Check attic for condensation signs — frost on sheathing, wet insulation
    (PNW temperature differential causes condensation in poorly ventilated attics)
[ ] Confirm attic insulation is not blocking soffit vents
[ ] Check for any evidence of animal entry — nesting material, droppings
[ ] Note insulation depth — R-38 minimum for Clark County attics
```

**Crawl Space**
```
[ ] Check for moisture or standing water — winter is peak risk period
[ ] Inspect vapor barrier — confirm intact and properly lapped
[ ] Check for frost or ice on pipes in crawl space
[ ] Look for any pest activity — rodents seek shelter in winter
[ ] Confirm crawl space access hatch is insulated and sealing properly
```

**Plumbing**
```
[ ] Confirm all hose bibs are winterized (from fall visit — verify)
[ ] Check under kitchen and bathroom sinks for any slow drips
[ ] Inspect water heater — no new rust, corrosion, or signs of distress
[ ] Check any pipes in unconditioned spaces — garage, utility room, crawl
[ ] Confirm client knows location of main water shut-off
```

**Exterior**
```
[ ] Check for ice dam formation if temperatures have been at freezing (look for icicles
    at eaves or staining on interior ceilings below roof — flag immediately if found)
[ ] Inspect any flat roof areas or low-slope transitions — pooling risk
[ ] Check downspout discharge — not blocked with ice or frozen debris
[ ] Walk perimeter — look for any storm damage from fall/early winter weather
```

**Annual Review Prep**
```
[ ] Pull all 4 seasonal logs from the year — review condition changes
[ ] Update Home Score (S9) to reflect year-end condition
[ ] Prepare annual summary for client review meeting:
    - Work completed this year with investment totals
    - Current condition by system
    - Recommended priorities for next year
[ ] Schedule annual review meeting with client (within 30 days of anniversary)
```

**PNW Winter Flags**
```
[ ] Ice dams — rare but possible in Clark County; check attic insulation adequacy
[ ] Crawl space moisture — peak risk; check every winter visit without exception
[ ] Attic condensation — common in PNW; check even if no visible roof issues
[ ] Frozen hose bibs — confirm winterization held; check after any hard freeze
```

---

## FIELD USE NOTES

```
At the start of every seasonal visit:
  1. Pull the client's last seasonal log from the portal before arriving
  2. Note any prior FLAG items — check those first
  3. Work through the full season checklist — don't skip items

At the end of every seasonal visit:
  1. Flag any new items in portal before leaving (S3 — Tracking SOP)
  2. Any CRITICAL finding → call client before leaving the property
  3. Update Home Score if any condition changed (S9)
  4. Send client summary email within 24 hours

Minimum photos per seasonal visit: 15–20
Label format: [System]-[Rating]-[Season-Year]
  Example: Gutters-FLAG-Fall2026
```

---

## RELATED SOPs
- S2 — Seasonal Inspection Log (uses these checklists as the inspection framework)
- S3 — Tracking / Client Portal (all checklist findings documented here)
- S4 — Prioritize (new FLAG items feed into the next prioritization review)
- S5 — Schedule (seasonal visit dates reference these scope definitions)
docs.title: P9 — SEASONAL VISIT CHECKLISTS (4x)

docs.body:
# S1 — BASELINE ASSESSMENT SOP
## 360° Method | Phase 1: AWARE | Step 1 of 9

```
SOP Name:     Step 1 — Baseline Assessment
360° Phase:   AWARE (Steps 1–3)
Work Type:    Predefined — runs once per new Proactive Path client
Who owns it:  Owner / CEO (Marcin — non-delegated until second trained assessor hired)
How often:    Once at enrollment. Never skipped. Never delegated to Lead Tech alone.
Last updated: 2026-05-28
```

---

## OBJECTIVE
Establish a complete, documented baseline of the home's condition across all 10 systems — creating the foundational record that every future visit, prioritization decision, and client conversation is built on.

---

## WHAT YOU NEED BEFORE STARTING

- Tablet or phone with camera (fully charged)
- Flashlight / headlamp (preferred — hands-free for attic and crawl space)
- Measuring tape
- Moisture meter
- HP inspection report template (printed or digital)
- HP-branded clipboard
- Safety glasses
- Client portal login — client profile created before arrival
- Confirmation that HVAC filters are visible, water heater accessible, electrical panel unlocked

---

## PRE-VISIT PREPARATION

**Scheduling & Client Communication**
- Schedule 2.5–3 hours minimum. Do not rush — this visit sets the tone for the entire relationship.
- Send client confirmation 48 hours prior: duration, areas to be accessed, what to prepare.
- Confirm client will be present for the full visit.

---

## ASSESSMENT PROTOCOL — 10 SYSTEMS

Complete every system below. Do not skip any system. For each: photograph, note condition, flag severity.

```
System                    What to Inspect
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1.  Roof & Gutters        Shingles, flashing, fascia, soffits, gutters, downspouts
2.  Exterior Envelope     Siding, trim, caulking, paint, windows, doors, weatherstripping
3.  Foundation & Drainage Grading, downspout discharge, visible cracks, crawl space moisture
4.  Plumbing              Water heater age/condition, supply/drain visible, exterior hose bibs
5.  HVAC                  Filter condition, unit age, visible ductwork, thermostat, condensate
6.  Electrical            Panel condition, GFCI presence, visible hazards, exterior outlets
7.  Attic                 Insulation level, ventilation, moisture signs, pest evidence
8.  Crawl Space           Vapor barrier, moisture, insulation, structural members, pests
9.  Interior Systems      Doors, windows, floors, walls, ceilings — deficiency-focused only
10. Exterior Structures   Decks, fences, outbuildings, driveways, walkways, retaining walls
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```

**Condition Rating — apply to each system:**
```
CRITICAL         Safety hazard or active failure. Requires immediate action.
NEEDS ATTENTION  Deteriorating. Will become Critical if not addressed within 12 months.
MONITOR          Early-stage concern. Track at next seasonal visit.
GOOD             No action needed at this time.
```

**Photo Protocol**
- Minimum 3 photos per system — one wide, one close-up, one deficiency detail if present.
- Label format: `[System]-[Rating]-[Date]` — e.g., `Roof-CRITICAL-2026-05-28`
- Upload to client portal before leaving site or by 6pm same day.

---

## PNW-SPECIFIC FLAGS — CHECK ON EVERY BASELINE

```
□ Roof moss or lichen (endemic in Clark County — check even newer roofs)
□ Gutter debris and downspout discharge directing toward foundation
□ Crawl space moisture or vapor barrier condition (PNW ground moisture is high)
□ Attic condensation signs (temperature differential causes this year-round)
□ Wood rot at any exterior penetration point (PNW moisture + wood = frequent failure)
□ HVAC filter — often neglected; note condition and interval client is using
□ Hose bibs — freeze protection status before winter
□ Deck ledger board condition (moisture trapped between deck and house)
```

---

## OUTPUT DELIVERABLES

```
□ All 10 systems inspected and rated (CRITICAL / NEEDS ATTENTION / MONITOR / GOOD)
□ Minimum 30 photos uploaded to client portal, labeled correctly
□ Baseline record saved in client portal under 'Assessment' tab — same day
□ 5-minute verbal debrief with client before leaving:
    - Top 2–3 findings, no pricing discussed
    - Set expectation: "Prioritization meeting in the next 5 business days"
□ Handoff to S2 (Seasonal Inspection Log): baseline record locked, monitoring begins
□ Handoff to S4 (Prioritize): all CRITICAL and NEEDS ATTENTION items flagged for roadmap
```

---

## DECISION POINTS

```
If a CRITICAL safety hazard is found → notify client verbally on-site before leaving.
  Flag in portal as CRITICAL. Schedule emergency response if client agrees.

If client is not present for any system (locked room, no ladder, etc.) →
  Note as "Not Inspected — Access Required" — do not skip, schedule follow-up.

If scope is significantly larger than expected (major deferred maintenance) →
  Debrief with Marcin before the prioritization meeting. Do not estimate on-site.
```

---

## HP MARGIN CHECK
*Not applicable to this SOP — assessment is included in Proactive Path enrollment.*

---

## DOCUMENTATION REQUIRED
```
□ All 10 system ratings entered in portal same day
□ Photos uploaded and labeled same day
□ Verbal debrief with client before leaving — noted in portal
□ Baseline record locked before Step 4 (Prioritize) meeting is scheduled
```

---

## COMMON MISTAKES
- Skipping a system because it "looked fine from outside" — never acceptable. Document GOOD.
- Taking photos without labeling — creates confusion in every future visit.
- Discussing pricing during the assessment — pricing happens in Step 4.
- Leaving without setting the next meeting (prioritization) — always leave with a date.

---

## HOW YOU KNOW YOU'RE DONE
All 10 systems are rated, photographed, and uploaded to the client portal. Client has received a verbal debrief. Prioritization meeting is scheduled. Baseline is locked.

---

## RELATED SOPs
- S2 — Seasonal Inspection Log (monitoring begins after baseline is locked)
- S3 — Tracking / Client Portal (documentation standard)
- S4 — Prioritize (uses baseline output to build the roadmap)
docs.title: S1 — BASELINE ASSESSMENT SOP

docs.body:
# S2 — INSPECT (SEASONAL INSPECTION LOG) SOP
## 360° Method | Phase 1: AWARE | Step 2 of 9

```
SOP Name:     Step 2 — Inspect (Seasonal Inspection Log)
360° Phase:   AWARE (Steps 1–3) — ongoing after baseline
Work Type:    Predefined — runs 4x per year per Proactive Path client
Who owns it:  Lead Technician (Owner until Lead Tech hired)
How often:    4x per year — Spring / Summer / Fall / Winter
Last updated: 2026-05-28
```

---

## OBJECTIVE
At each seasonal visit, document what has changed since the last inspection — updating the home's condition record so the Prioritize step (S4) always works from current data, not baseline data that's 6–12 months stale.

---

## CRITICAL DISTINCTION — BASELINE vs. SEASONAL LOG

```
Step 1 (Baseline):       One-time. Establishes the home's full condition from zero.
                         Covers all 10 systems in depth. Owner-led always.

Step 2 (Seasonal Log):   Recurring. Answers one question: what changed since last time?
                         Focuses on change detection, not full re-inspection.
                         Uses the baseline as the reference point every time.
```

---

## WHAT YOU NEED BEFORE STARTING

- Prior seasonal log or baseline record pulled up in client portal
- Seasonal checklist for the current season (see S5 — Schedule for seasonal task lists)
- Tablet or phone with camera (fully charged)
- Client portal login — visit log open before arriving

---

## INSPECTION PROTOCOL

**The Seasonal Log answers three questions for each system:**
1. Has the condition rating changed since the last visit?
2. Is there anything new that wasn't present at baseline or last visit?
3. Have any previously flagged items worsened, improved, or been resolved?

**Systems to check at every seasonal visit:**
```
Priority Check Systems (every visit — no exceptions):
  □ Roof & Gutters
  □ Exterior Envelope (caulking, paint, penetrations)
  □ Foundation & Drainage
  □ HVAC (filter change + condition check)
  □ Any open CRITICAL or NEEDS ATTENTION items from prior log

Season-Specific Systems (see S5 — Schedule for per-season task detail):
  Spring:  Roof post-winter, gutters post-debris season, pest entry points, exterior wood
  Summer:  HVAC performance, deck/outdoor structures, irrigation if present
  Fall:    Gutter clearing, weatherstripping, hose bib winterization, heating prep
  Winter:  Attic condensation, crawl space moisture, heat loss signals, ice dam risk
```

---

## CONDITION LOG ENTRY FORMAT

For each system inspected, enter in client portal:

```
System:           [System Name]
Prior Rating:     [Rating from last log]
Current Rating:   [CRITICAL / NEEDS ATTENTION / MONITOR / GOOD]
Change:           [Improved / Unchanged / Worsened / New Finding]
Notes:            [What specifically was observed — 1–3 sentences]
Action Required:  [Yes → flag for S4 Prioritize / No → note and close]
```

**Rating change triggers:**
```
Any GOOD → MONITOR or worse:   Add to portal roadmap, flag for next S4 review
Any MONITOR → NEEDS ATTENTION: Flag immediately — schedule S4 prioritization meeting
Any rating → CRITICAL:         Call client same day before leaving the property
```

---

## PHOTO PROTOCOL

- Minimum 2 photos per system inspected.
- Any condition change: before/after or comparison photo vs. prior visit.
- Label: `[System]-[Rating]-[Season-Year]` — e.g., `Gutters-NEEDSATTENTION-Fall2026`
- Upload to portal before leaving site or by 6pm same day.

---

## OUTPUT DELIVERABLES

```
□ Seasonal log entry completed in portal for every system checked
□ All condition changes flagged and categorized
□ Photos uploaded and labeled same day
□ Any new CRITICAL items: client notified by phone before leaving
□ Any new NEEDS ATTENTION items: added to portal roadmap, flagged for S4
□ Handoff to S3 (Tracking): log entry complete, portal updated
□ Handoff to S4 (Prioritize): new findings queued for next prioritization review
□ Visit summary email sent to client within 24 hours (see S3)
```

---

## DECISION POINTS

```
If condition has worsened significantly → call client immediately. Do not wait for summary email.

If you find something outside your skill set to assess accurately (e.g., structural concern,
electrical issue) → note as "Requires Specialist Assessment," flag in portal, notify Marcin.

If a prior roadmap item is now WORSE despite being scheduled → IDS trigger.
  Surface at next L10. Do not absorb or minimize.
```

---

## HP MARGIN CHECK
*Not applicable to this SOP — seasonal visits are included in Proactive Path subscription.*

---

## DOCUMENTATION REQUIRED
```
□ Portal log entry — same day, every system checked
□ Photos uploaded and labeled — same day
□ Client summary email — within 24 hours
□ Any roadmap updates entered before leaving site
```

---

## COMMON MISTAKES
- Comparing to baseline instead of the last seasonal log — use the most recent record.
- Skipping systems because they "looked fine last time" — check and document GOOD if no change.
- Not flagging condition changes — even a MONITOR → MONITOR with visible progression needs a note.
- Leaving without updating the portal — a visit without documentation did not happen.

---

## HOW YOU KNOW YOU'RE DONE
Portal log entry is complete for all systems checked. Client summary email sent within 24 hours. Any new findings are flagged and queued for S4 Prioritize.

---

## RELATED SOPs
- S1 — Baseline Assessment (the reference record this log measures change against)
- S3 — Tracking / Client Portal (documentation standards)
- S4 — Prioritize (receives new findings from this log)
- S5 — Schedule (per-season task checklists)
docs.title: S2 — INSPECT (SEASONAL INSPECTION LOG) SOP

docs.body:
# S3 — TRACKING (CLIENT PORTAL) SOP
## 360° Method | Phase 1: AWARE | Step 3 of 9

```
SOP Name:     Step 3 — Tracking / Client Portal Update Protocol
360° Phase:   AWARE (Steps 1–3) — active throughout all phases
Work Type:    Predefined — triggers after every field visit or project completion
Who owns it:  Lead Technician (field documentation) / Office (portal QC)
How often:    After every visit, every project completion, every condition change
Last updated: 2026-05-28
```

---

## OBJECTIVE
The client portal is HP's primary value-delivery tool. Clients see everything entered. The portal is what makes the 360° Method visible — without it, HP is just another contractor. A visit without portal documentation did not happen.

---

## WHAT YOU NEED BEFORE STARTING

- Client portal login
- Completed seasonal log or visit notes (S2) or completed scope documentation (S6)
- Photos from the visit — labeled and ready to upload
- Roadmap status for any items completed or newly discovered

---

## ENTRY TIMELINE STANDARDS

```
Trigger                              Portal Update Due
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Any field visit (seasonal or project) Same day — before 6pm
Baseline assessment                  Same day — before 6pm
New CRITICAL finding discovered      Immediately — call client first, then log
Roadmap item completed               Same day as completion
Scope change discovered on-site      Before leaving property — note in portal
Annual review meeting                Within 24 hours of meeting
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```

---

## VISIT LOG ENTRY PROTOCOL

Complete in this sequence for every visit:

```
Step 1:  Log in → select client → "Add Visit Log"
Step 2:  Enter: visit date, type (Seasonal/Execution/Assessment/Emergency), duration
Step 3:  Summary narrative (3–5 sentences):
           - What was checked
           - What was found
           - What was done
           - What is pending
Step 4:  Check off all items from the seasonal checklist or job scope
Step 5:  Flag any deficiencies as "Discovered" — categorize: NOW / SOON / WAIT
Step 6:  Note any roadmap items completed this visit — change status to "Complete"
Step 7:  Save and preview — review before submitting
```

---

## PHOTO UPLOAD PROTOCOL

```
Step 1:  Minimum 10 photos per visit; seasonal visits should have 15–20
Step 2:  Label each photo: [Category]-[Description]-[Season/Date]
           Good: "Gutters-Post-Cleaning-Spring2026"
           Good: "Roof-Moss-NEEDSATTENTION-Spring2026"
           Bad:  "IMG_4872.jpg" — never acceptable
Step 3:  Tag photo to the relevant visit log
Step 4:  Any deficiency photo → mark as "Deficiency" — client sees a flag
Step 5:  Before/after for completed work → upload both, label clearly
```

---

## ROADMAP UPDATE PROTOCOL

```
Completed item:   Change status to "Complete." Add completion date.
                  Attach invoice reference number.

New discovery:    Add new roadmap item. Assign priority (NOW/SOON/WAIT).
                  Include ROM cost range. Note source visit.

Timing change:    Update "Target Date" field. Add a note explaining the change.

Client notification:
  Portal auto-notifies client of updates — no separate email needed UNLESS:
  - The item is NOW priority
  - The condition worsened significantly
  - A project was completed (send personal summary email within 24 hrs)
```

---

## ACCURACY STANDARDS

```
□ Every system inspected has an entry — even if GOOD with no change
□ All photos labeled — no unnamed files
□ Roadmap reflects current status — nothing marked "Scheduled" that isn't actually scheduled
□ Cost ranges in portal are ROM only — never firm quotes without a written estimate
□ No dollar amounts without a signed estimate in the file
```

---

## DECISION POINTS

```
If a CRITICAL item is found → call client before portal entry. Do not let the portal
  notification be how they find out about a safety issue.

If a prior entry has an error → correct it and add a timestamped correction note.
  Never delete entries.

If client disputes a portal record → flag for Marcin. Do not edit without owner review.
```

---

## HP MARGIN CHECK
*Not applicable — documentation step. No pricing in portal without a signed estimate.*

---

## DOCUMENTATION REQUIRED
```
□ Visit log entry — every visit, same day
□ Photos uploaded and labeled — every visit, same day
□ Roadmap status current — every completed or newly discovered item updated
□ Client summary email sent within 24 hours for seasonal visits and project completions
```

---

## COMMON MISTAKES
- Skipping the portal after a "quick visit" — no exceptions. Every visit gets an entry.
- Using photo filenames from the camera — always relabel before uploading.
- Marking roadmap items "Complete" without attaching the invoice reference.
- Entering cost estimates as firm prices — ROM ranges only unless estimate is signed.

---

## HOW YOU KNOW YOU'RE DONE
Visit log entry is submitted, photos are labeled and uploaded, roadmap reflects current status, and client has received a summary communication if required.

---

## RELATED SOPs
- S1 — Baseline Assessment (first portal entries are made during baseline)
- S2 — Seasonal Inspection Log (every log entry is documented here)
- S4 — Prioritize (uses portal roadmap data to run prioritization)
- S6 — Execute (every completed item is documented here)
docs.title: S3 — TRACKING (CLIENT PORTAL) SOP

docs.body:
# S4 — PRIORITIZE SOP
## 360° Method | Phase 2: ACT | Step 4 of 9

```
SOP Name:     Step 4 — Prioritize
360° Phase:   ACT (Steps 4–6)
Work Type:    Predefined — runs after baseline and after each seasonal inspection
Who owns it:  Owner / CEO (Marcin) — prioritization meeting with client is owner-led
How often:    Once after baseline. Reviewed and updated after each seasonal log.
Last updated: 2026-05-28
```

---

## OBJECTIVE
Take everything found in the assessment and inspection logs — system conditions, new findings, client goals, budget, timeline, and seasonal cadence — and produce a single prioritized action plan the client understands and has approved. The Priority Roadmap is the core deliverable of the ACT phase and the foundation of every execution decision.

---

## WHAT THIS STEP DOES THAT A SIMPLE LIST DOESN'T

Prioritization at HP is not just sorting by severity. It is a holistic review of:
- **Urgency** — how critical is the condition right now?
- **Client budget** — what can they act on this year vs. next?
- **Client goals** — are they planning to sell, refinance, or stay long-term?
- **Seasonal timing** — what has to happen before winter? Before spring moisture season?
- **One-off requests** — what has the client asked about that isn't on the inspection list?
- **Sequencing logic** — what must be done before something else can be done?

The output is a roadmap the client has agreed to — not a list of everything that's wrong.

---

## WHAT YOU NEED BEFORE STARTING

- Client portal open — all inspection findings and condition ratings current (S2, S3 complete)
- Rough order of magnitude (ROM) cost ranges for each flagged item
- Client's stated goals and timeline (from enrollment conversation or last annual review)
- Marcin present — this meeting is owner-led

---

## THE NOW / SOON / WAIT FRAMEWORK

```
NOW    Act within 30 days
       Criteria: Safety hazard / active failure / will cause significantly higher cost
       if delayed / client has explicitly asked to act immediately
       Examples: CRITICAL roof leak, failed water heater, exposed electrical

SOON   Act within 3–12 months
       Criteria: NEEDS ATTENTION condition / deteriorating but not failing /
       budget or seasonal timing makes immediate action impractical
       Examples: Deck board replacement, exterior repainting, HVAC service

WAIT   Monitor — act when triggered
       Criteria: MONITOR condition / functional but aging /
       no immediate consequence to deferring
       Examples: Roof at 15 years with no visible damage, aging water heater
                 functioning normally, fence showing minor weathering
```

---

## COST RANGING RULES

```
□ All roadmap entries use ROM (rough order of magnitude) ranges — never firm bids
□ ROM ranges presented as: "$X,XXX–$X,XXX depending on scope"
□ Do NOT present cost ranges to client as quotes — verbal or written
□ Firm estimates only come from the Estimating & Margin Audit SOP (P4)
   after scope is approved and S5 (Schedule) is confirmed
□ Never expose markup, internal rates, or cost math
```

---

## PRIORITIZATION MEETING PROTOCOL

Run in this sequence for every new roadmap delivery and every annual review:

```
Step 1:  Schedule 30-minute Prioritization Meeting (in-person or video)
           - New clients: within 5 business days of baseline assessment
           - Annual reviews: within 2 weeks of the anniversary seasonal visit

Step 2:  Before the meeting — pull current portal data:
           - All CRITICAL and NEEDS ATTENTION items from S1 and S2
           - All open SOON and WAIT items from prior roadmap
           - Any client-initiated requests not yet on the roadmap

Step 3:  Open the meeting with a single framing statement:
           "This is your home's current priority list. We're going to walk through
           it together, account for your budget and goals, and agree on what
           happens this year, what we're planning for next year, and what we're
           watching."

Step 4:  Walk through NOW items first — confirm client understands urgency and cost range

Step 5:  Walk through SOON items — discuss timing and budget alignment
           Ask: "Is there anything here you'd like to move to NOW?"
           Ask: "What's your rough budget for the next 12 months?"

Step 6:  Note any one-off client requests not yet in the portal — add to roadmap

Step 7:  Confirm the agreed priority sequence — client verbal alignment required

Step 8:  Enter confirmed roadmap in client portal under 'Priority Roadmap' tab

Step 9:  Get written acknowledgment: send follow-up email — "Does this capture
           everything we discussed?" — client reply is the approval record

Step 10: Hand off NOW items to S5 (Schedule) — get dates on the calendar
```

---

## ROADMAP DOCUMENT FORMAT

```
Priority  Item                     System      ROM Range         Target
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
NOW       [Item description]       [System]    $X,XXX–$X,XXX    [Month/Year]
SOON      [Item description]       [System]    $X,XXX–$X,XXX    [Quarter/Year]
WAIT      [Item description]       [System]    $X,XXX–$X,XXX    Monitor only
```

---

## DECISION POINTS

```
If client pushes back on a NOW item (doesn't want to spend) →
  Document the conversation in portal. Note: "Client deferred — informed of risk."
  Never remove a CRITICAL item from the roadmap — only change status with client sign-off.

If client's budget is significantly lower than the NOW items require →
  Sequence the most critical first. Present phasing options.
  Never promise work that can't be executed at margin — check ROM against 30%/40% floors.

If client adds a request that HP cannot deliver at margin →
  Do not commit on-site. Bring back a proper estimate (P4 — Estimating SOP).
```

---

## HP MARGIN CHECK
```
□ All ROM ranges in roadmap account for minimum 30% GM (standard jobs)
□ Any scope with hard cost under $2,000 → minimum 40% GM — adjust ROM range accordingly
□ Never present a cost range that would require below-floor margin to deliver
□ Firm pricing only after Estimating & Margin Audit SOP (P4) is run
```

---

## DOCUMENTATION REQUIRED
```
□ Completed roadmap in client portal — every item rated NOW/SOON/WAIT
□ Meeting notes entered in portal — key decisions and client preferences documented
□ Client acknowledgment email received — file in portal
□ NOW items handed off to S5 (Schedule) — target dates confirmed
```

---

## COMMON MISTAKES
- Presenting ROM ranges as quotes — they are estimates only.
- Removing CRITICAL items from the roadmap because the client doesn't want to act.
- Skipping the annual roadmap review — this is HP's primary retention tool.
- Letting the roadmap go stale — it must reflect actual current conditions after every S2 log.

---

## HOW YOU KNOW YOU'RE DONE
Client has reviewed and acknowledged the roadmap in writing. All items are rated and in the portal. NOW items have target dates and are handed to S5 (Schedule).

---

## RELATED SOPs
- S2 — Seasonal Inspection Log (primary data source for roadmap updates)
- S3 — Tracking / Client Portal (roadmap lives here)
- S5 — Schedule (receives NOW items for calendar coordination)
- P4 — Estimating & Margin Audit SOP (firm pricing after scope is approved)
docs.title: S4 — PRIORITIZE SOP

docs.body:
# S5 — SCHEDULE SOP
## 360° Method | Phase 2: ACT | Step 5 of 9

```
SOP Name:     Step 5 — Schedule
360° Phase:   ACT (Steps 4–6)
Work Type:    Predefined — runs after every prioritization and for all one-off jobs
Who owns it:  Office / Estimator (Owner until Office hired)
How often:    4x per year for seasonal cadence + as needed for project scheduling
Last updated: 2026-05-28
```

---

## OBJECTIVE
Confirm dates with the client for both the seasonal maintenance cadence and any one-off project work — so HP and the client are aligned on what happens when, and execution (S6) is never blocked by a scheduling gap.

---

## TWO SCHEDULING TRACKS — ALWAYS RUNNING IN PARALLEL

```
Track 1: Seasonal Cadence          Track 2: Project / One-Off Jobs
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━   ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
4 visits per year, every year.     Items from the Priority Roadmap (S4).
Set annually at enrollment and     Each item has a target window from the roadmap.
confirmed at each annual review.   Schedule when: scope is approved + estimate signed.
Client must have dates on          Coordinate with: Lead Tech availability,
the calendar — no "sometime        sub availability (if trade required), client window.
in the spring."
```

---

## SEASONAL CADENCE SCHEDULING

**Set the annual calendar at enrollment.** Do not leave it open-ended.

```
Season        Target Month(s)        Trigger to Schedule
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Spring        March – April          Post-winter inspection + gutter clearing
Summer        June – July            Exterior systems, HVAC performance check
Fall          September – October    Pre-winter prep, gutter clearing, weatherization
Winter        December – January     Interior systems, heating, condensation check
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```

**Scheduling process for seasonal visits:**

```
Step 1:  At enrollment — set all 4 seasonal visit dates for the first year.
           Present 2–3 date options per season. Client picks.
           Enter all 4 confirmed dates in client portal and HP calendar.

Step 2:  Send calendar invite (or confirmation email) to client for each date.
           Include: approximate duration (2–3 hrs), what to expect, access needed.

Step 3:  At each annual review — confirm next year's seasonal calendar.
           Adjust months based on client preferences or prior-year experience.

Step 4:  Reminder: send 48-hour confirmation before every seasonal visit.
           If client needs to reschedule → rebook within same season, not the next.
```

---

## PROJECT / ONE-OFF SCHEDULING

**Triggered by:** S4 (Prioritize) confirming a NOW or SOON item with an approved ROM range and client sign-off.

```
Step 1:  Confirm estimate is signed (P4 — Estimating & Margin Audit SOP complete).
           Do NOT schedule project work without a signed estimate.

Step 2:  Check Lead Tech availability (or Owner, until Lead Tech hired).
           If sub required → check sub availability first, then set client date.

Step 3:  Present 2–3 date windows to client. Get confirmation.

Step 4:  Enter confirmed date in client portal under the roadmap item.
           Update roadmap item status: WAIT/SOON → "Scheduled"

Step 5:  For trade work (plumbing, electrical, HVAC) — confirm sub booking separately.
           Sub must be confirmed before client date is locked.

Step 6:  Send client confirmation: scope summary, date/time, duration estimate,
           access requirements, payment terms reminder.

Step 7:  Send 24-hour reminder to client on the day before.
```

---

## SCHEDULING CONFLICTS AND RESCHEDULES

```
If client needs to reschedule a seasonal visit →
  Rebook within the same season. Do not defer to next season — the cadence must hold.
  Update portal and send new confirmation.

If HP needs to reschedule (Lead Tech sick, sub unavailable) →
  Call client directly — do not send a text or email first for a cancellation.
  Offer 2 new dates within 5 business days.
  Log the reschedule in portal with reason.

If a sub cancels and a project date is at risk →
  Notify client immediately. Do not wait to see if you can find a replacement.
  Find backup sub or adjust scope. Never leave a client waiting without communication.
```

---

## SCHEDULING RULES

```
□ No seasonal visit left open — every PP client has dates on the calendar at all times
□ No project work scheduled without a signed estimate
□ Sub availability confirmed before client date is locked on trade work
□ All confirmed dates in client portal — not just in personal calendar
□ Reschedules within same season for seasonal visits — no deferrals
```

---

## HP MARGIN CHECK
*Not applicable — this SOP governs scheduling, not pricing. Pricing is handled in P4 before scheduling begins.*

---

## DOCUMENTATION REQUIRED
```
□ All 4 seasonal visit dates in client portal and HP calendar — confirmed annually
□ Every project date entered in portal under the relevant roadmap item
□ Client confirmation email or written acknowledgment for every scheduled date
□ Reschedules logged in portal with reason and new date
```

---

## COMMON MISTAKES
- Leaving seasonal visits as "sometime in the spring" — always get a specific date.
- Scheduling project work before the estimate is signed — creates scope and margin risk.
- Booking a sub without confirming client date first (or vice versa) — always confirm both.
- Not updating portal when dates change — stale scheduling data causes delivery failures.

---

## HOW YOU KNOW YOU'RE DONE
Every active PP client has all 4 seasonal visit dates confirmed on the calendar. Every approved project scope has a scheduled date in the portal with client confirmation.

---

## RELATED SOPs
- S4 — Prioritize (hands off NOW/SOON items for scheduling)
- S6 — Execute (receives confirmed schedule and executes)
- S2 — Seasonal Inspection Log (the work that happens at each seasonal visit)
- P4 — Estimating & Margin Audit SOP (must be complete before project is scheduled)
docs.title: S5 — SCHEDULE SOP

docs.body:
# S6 — EXECUTE SOP
## 360° Method | Phase 2: ACT | Step 6 of 9

```
SOP Name:     Step 6 — Execute
360° Phase:   ACT (Steps 4–6)
Work Type:    Predefined — governs all approved work items from the schedule
Who owns it:  Lead Technician + Office / Estimator
How often:    Per job — every roadmap execution item, every project scope
Last updated: 2026-05-28
```

---

## OBJECTIVE
Deliver every approved scope item with proper written scope, margin-verified pricing, client sign-off, clean execution, and same-day documentation — every time. This SOP governs the Scope → Estimate → Approve → Execute → Document cycle.

---

## WHAT YOU NEED BEFORE STARTING

- Approved roadmap item from S4 (Prioritize) with client acknowledgment
- Confirmed schedule date from S5 (Schedule)
- Signed estimate with margin audit complete (P4 — Estimating & Margin Audit SOP)
- If sub required: sub confirmed for the same date

---

## THE 9-STEP EXECUTION CYCLE

Follow in sequence. Do not skip steps. Each step has a required output before moving to the next.

```
Step 1 — SCOPE
  Write the job scope before any client communication.
  Scope must include: what will be done, what will NOT be done, materials,
  duration estimate, who performs the work (HP or named sub trade).
  Output: Written scope document.

Step 2 — ESTIMATE
  Run P4 — Estimating & Margin Audit SOP on the scope.
  Hard cost under $2,000 → 40% GM minimum.
  Hard cost over $2,000 → 30% GM minimum.
  Never expose markup, internal rates, or cost math.
  Output: Client-facing estimate (plain text — scope bullets, total investment,
  payment terms). Internal margin audit on file.

Step 3 — APPROVE
  Send estimate to client. Get written approval (email or portal sign-off).
  Do not schedule or order materials without written approval.
  Output: Client approval on file in portal.

Step 4 — PERMIT CHECK
  Determine if work requires a Clark County permit.
  Pull permit if required — do not begin permitted work without approval.
  Output: Permit on file or documented exemption.

Step 5 — MATERIALS & SUB COORDINATION
  Order materials after client approval.
  Confirm sub date and scope if trade work is involved.
  Sub must have: job brief, scope summary, HP conduct expectations.
  Output: Materials confirmed. Sub confirmed (if applicable).

Step 6 — EXECUTE
  Arrive on time. Site clean at all times during work.
  HP-branded appearance. No personal phone use visible to client.
  If scope change discovered on-site → stop, document, call client before proceeding.
  Never absorb a scope change without written approval and updated estimate.
  Output: Work completed to HP quality standard.

Step 7 — CLIENT SIGN-OFF
  Walk client through completed work before leaving.
  Get verbal or written confirmation: "Does this meet your expectations?"
  If any concern: address on-site or schedule resolution — do not leave unresolved.
  Output: Client sign-off documented in portal.

Step 8 — DOCUMENT
  Same-day portal update (S3 — Tracking SOP):
    - Visit log entry with scope completed and any notes
    - Before/after photos — labeled
    - Roadmap item status → "Complete" with completion date and invoice reference
  Output: Portal updated same day.

Step 9 — INVOICE
  Run margin check before invoicing — confirm final cost vs. estimate.
  If final hard cost exceeds estimate → flag to Marcin before invoicing.
  Send invoice. Note payment terms. Log in portal.
  Output: Invoice sent with all documentation attached.
```

---

## SCOPE CHANGE PROTOCOL

```
If hidden damage or new scope is discovered on-site:
  1. Stop work on the affected area.
  2. Document with photos immediately.
  3. Call client — describe what was found, what it means, what the options are.
  4. Get verbal approval to proceed with expanded scope.
  5. Send updated written estimate and get written sign-off before continuing.
  6. Update portal with scope change note and new estimate reference.

Never absorb a scope change. Never proceed with undocumented expanded work.
```

---

## MARGIN RULES

```
Standard job (hard cost ≥ $2,000):    Minimum 30% gross margin
Small job (hard cost < $2,000):       Minimum 40% gross margin
Internal labor cost rate:             $100/hr [INTERNAL — never share]
Customer billing rate (labor):        $150/hr [INTERNAL — never share]
Sub labor cost rate:                  Varies — Sub #1 (Carpenter): $100/hr
  ⚠ Sub #1 at $100/hr + billing $150/hr = 33% GM → FAILS small job floor.
     Do not use Sub #1 on jobs with hard cost under $2,000 without adjusting billing rate.

IDS trigger: Any job finishing below margin floor — flag same day.
```

---

## PERMIT MATRIX — CLARK COUNTY, WA

```
Typically Requires Permit:           Typically Exempt:
  Structural work                      Repair in kind (same materials)
  Electrical panel or new circuits     Painting, flooring, cabinets
  Plumbing rough-in changes            Minor drywall repair
  HVAC new installation                Deck repair (not replacement)
  Additions or expansions              Fencing under 6 feet
  Window/door structural changes

When in doubt: call Clark County Building Dept before starting.
  Clark County Permit Center: (564) 397-2375
```

---

## COMMUNICATION RULES

```
□ Client communication at three points minimum: before (scope confirm), during
  (if scope change), after (sign-off + invoice)
□ All client communication routes through HP — subs do not contact client directly
□ Any surprise finding → phone call, not text
□ Completion summary email within 24 hours of any project job
```

---

## HP MARGIN CHECK
```
□ P4 Estimating SOP complete before client receives estimate
□ Margin floor met: 30% standard / 40% small job
□ Final cost vs. estimate reviewed before invoicing — every job
□ Any below-floor result → IDS trigger same day
```

---

## DOCUMENTATION REQUIRED
```
□ Written scope before estimate is sent
□ Signed estimate and client approval on file
□ Permit documentation (or exemption documented)
□ Same-day portal update: visit log, photos, roadmap status
□ Client sign-off documented
□ Invoice sent with margin audit on file
```

---

## COMMON MISTAKES
- Starting work before written client approval — never acceptable.
- Absorbing scope changes without updated estimate — destroys margin and sets precedent.
- Leaving site without portal update — if it isn't documented, HP can't bill it or defend it.
- Invoicing before checking final margin — a job that looked fine can fail the floor on cost overruns.

---

## HOW YOU KNOW YOU'RE DONE
Scope completed to HP standard. Client sign-off documented. Portal updated same day. Invoice sent with margin audit confirmed.

---

## RELATED SOPs
- S4 — Prioritize (source of all approved work items)
- S5 — Schedule (confirmed dates feed this SOP)
- S3 — Tracking / Client Portal (every execution item documented here)
- P4 — Estimating & Margin Audit SOP (must precede every estimate)
- S7 — Preservation Projects (uses this same cycle for Phase 3 work)
docs.title: S6 — EXECUTE SOP

docs.body:
# S7 — PRESERVATION PROJECTS SOP
## 360° Method | Phase 3: ADVANCE | Step 7 of 9

```
SOP Name:     Step 7 — Preservation Projects
360° Phase:   ADVANCE (Steps 7–9)
Work Type:    Predefined — applies to clients 12+ months in the Proactive Path
Who owns it:  Owner / CEO (Marcin) + Lead Technician
How often:    As identified during seasonal inspections and annual reviews
```
