```
Last updated: 2026-05-28
```

---

## OBJECTIVE
Identify home systems approaching end-of-life and act before failure — extending system life, reducing emergency costs, and protecting the client's asset through proactive investment rather than reactive repair.

---

## WHAT IS A PRESERVATION PROJECT?
A preservation project is proactive work done before failure. It is not a repair — it is a deliberate intervention that prevents the need for a more expensive repair or replacement. The goal is to identify deteriorating systems and act during the preservation window, before the client experiences a failure event.

Phase 3 applies to clients who have been with Handy Pioneers for 12+ months. By this stage, HP has established a complete baseline, run at least 4 seasonal visits, executed roadmap items, and earned the trust needed for deeper preservation conversations.

---

## LIFE-EXTENSION BENCHMARKS

Use to identify when each system enters its preservation window:

```
System                  Typical Lifespan    Preservation Window
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Asphalt Roof            20–25 years         15–18 years
Composite Decking       15–25 years         12–18 years
Pressure-Treated Deck   10–15 years         8–12 years
Wood Fence              10–15 years         8–12 years
Exterior Paint          7–10 years          6–8 years
HVAC System             15–20 years         12–15 years
Water Heater            10–12 years         8–10 years
Caulking/Sealants       5–7 years           4–5 years
Crawl Space Vapor Barr. 10–20 years         10 years (inspect annually)
Gutters (aluminum)      20–30 years         Inspect for pitch/sag at 15 years
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```

---

## PRESERVATION PROJECT IDENTIFICATION PROTOCOL

```
Step 1:  At each seasonal visit (S2) — flag any system entering its preservation window.
           Note in portal under the system: "Approaching preservation window."

Step 2:  At annual review (S4 — Prioritize) — review all systems with preservation flags.
           Present the life-extension window and cost comparison:
           "Preserve now for $X,XXX or replace later for $XX,XXX."

Step 3:  Qualify the project:
           □ Is the system in its preservation window? (not too early, not too late)
           □ Is preservation technically viable, or is replacement more appropriate?
           □ Does the client's goal (stay vs. sell vs. refinance) support the investment?

Step 4:  Build the scope. Run the estimating workflow (P4 — Estimating SOP).
           Present as investment, not cost. Use "Good / Better / Best" option structure
           where scope allows — gives client agency, protects HP margin on the middle option.

Step 5:  Get client approval. Schedule through S5 (Schedule).

Step 6:  Execute through S6 (Execute). Document completion in portal.
           Update system condition rating and preservation flag to "Complete."
```

---

## PRESERVATION vs. REACTIVE CRITERIA

```
Preservation (Step 7):        Reactive (S6 Execution only):
  Proactive — within window     Failure has already occurred
  Planned — not urgent          Urgent — NOW priority
  Investment framing applies    Repair framing — get it fixed
  Present options               One correct action
  Phase 3 clients only          Any client, any phase
```

---

## SCOPE AND ESTIMATING RULES

All preservation projects go through the full HP estimating workflow (P4). No exceptions.

```
Margin floors:     30% GM minimum (standard). 40% GM minimum (hard cost under $2,000).
Framing:           Always use "investment" — never the word "cost" with clients.
Option structure:  Good / Better / Best where applicable.
                   Protects margin on the recommended (Better) option.
```

---

## DOCUMENTATION REQUIRED
```
□ Preservation flag noted in portal at the seasonal visit when window is entered
□ Life-extension conversation documented at annual review
□ Client approval on file before scope begins
□ Completion documented: system condition updated, preservation flag closed
□ Before/after photos uploaded and labeled
□ Invoice with margin audit on file
```

---

## COMMON MISTAKES
- Proposing preservation to clients before 12 months — trust isn't earned yet.
- Waiting until failure to flag preservation windows — defeats the entire value proposition.
- Using "cost" language — always "investment" or "protection for your asset."
- Skipping the "Good / Better / Best" structure — leaves money on the table.

---

## HOW YOU KNOW YOU'RE DONE
Preservation project scoped, estimated, approved, executed, and documented. System condition rating updated in portal. Client understands the work done and its long-term value.

---

## RELATED SOPs
- S2 — Seasonal Inspection Log (flags preservation windows as they approach)
- S4 — Prioritize (preservation items appear in the annual roadmap review)
- S6 — Execute (preservation projects use the same execution cycle)
- P4 — Estimating & Margin Audit SOP
docs.title: S7 — PRESERVATION PROJECTS SOP

docs.body:
# S8 — STRATEGIC UPGRADES SOP
## 360° Method | Phase 3: ADVANCE | Step 8 of 9

```
SOP Name:     Step 8 — Strategic Upgrades
360° Phase:   ADVANCE (Steps 7–9)
Work Type:    Predefined — applies to clients 12+ months in the Proactive Path
Who owns it:  Owner / CEO (Marcin) + Lead Technician
How often:    As identified and qualified during annual reviews
Last updated: 2026-05-28
```

---

## OBJECTIVE
Identify and present ROI-driven home improvements that go beyond maintenance and preservation — adding function, value, or appeal to the home in alignment with the client's goals. Strategic upgrades are the highest-value work HP delivers.

---

## WHAT IS A STRATEGIC UPGRADE?
A strategic upgrade is elective work that goes beyond routine maintenance or preservation. It adds function, value, or appeal to the home and is positioned as a strategic investment aligned with the client's stated goals. Unlike preservation (preventing loss), upgrades are about creating gain — equity, livability, or marketability.

---

## THREE CLIENT GOAL PROFILES

Present upgrades through the lens of the client's primary goal:

```
Goal Profile 1 — SELL (planning to sell within 1–3 years)
  Frame upgrades as: "What moves the needle on price and days on market?"
  High-ROI focus: kitchen/bath updates, curb appeal, energy efficiency, condition scores
  Reference: local MLS data, agent feedback HP has gathered

Goal Profile 2 — REFINANCE (building equity, planning to cash out)
  Frame upgrades as: "What adds appraised value?"
  Focus: permitted improvements, square footage, systems modernization
  Reference: comparable sold properties, appraiser feedback

Goal Profile 3 — STAY (long-term ownership, livability focus)
  Frame upgrades as: "What makes this home work better for your life?"
  Focus: lifestyle improvements, energy cost reduction, smart home, accessibility
  No urgency pressure — timeline is client-driven
```

---

## ROI REFERENCE TABLE (PNW / Clark County Context)

```
Upgrade Type                     Typical ROI Range    Client Goal Fit
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Minor kitchen update              80–100%              Sell / Refinance
Bathroom refresh                  70–90%               Sell / Refinance
Exterior paint + curb appeal      80–100%              Sell
Deck replacement / addition       60–80%               Sell / Stay
Energy efficiency upgrades        Variable             Stay / Refinance
Smart home (thermostat, security) Low financial ROI    Stay
Finished basement                 50–75%               Refinance / Stay
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```

---

## UPGRADE IDENTIFICATION PROTOCOL

```
Step 1:  At annual review — review client's current stated goal (Sell/Refinance/Stay).
           Note: goals change. Ask directly each year: "Has anything changed in your
           plans for the home?"

Step 2:  Review the home's current condition and what has been preserved/maintained.
           Identify gaps between current state and goal-state.

Step 3:  Identify 1–2 upgrade opportunities aligned with the client's goal.
           Do not present more than 2 upgrades simultaneously — clients disengage.

Step 4:  Build the presentation:
           - One-paragraph context: "Based on your goal of [X], here's what we're seeing."
           - Three options: Core / Enhanced / Premium
           - Each option: scope summary, investment range, expected impact
           - One recommendation (Enhanced is typically the right answer)

Step 5:  Run the estimate (P4 — Estimating & Margin Audit SOP) on the recommended option.
           Present ROM ranges for the other two options.

Step 6:  Get client approval. Allow decision time — no pressure.
           Space major upgrades 90+ days apart unless client initiates faster timeline.

Step 7:  Schedule through S5. Execute through S6. Document in portal.
```

---

## UPGRADE PROPOSAL STANDARDS

```
Always present three options: Core / Enhanced / Premium
  Core:      Minimum viable scope. Addresses the need, modest investment.
  Enhanced:  Recommended. Balances scope, quality, and investment.
  Premium:   Full scope. Best outcome, highest investment.

Volume limit:     Never present more than 2 upgrades in one conversation.
Spacing rule:     90+ days between major upgrade proposals unless client drives it.
No upsell framing: "Here's what I recommend" — not "you should really do this."
```

---

## ESTIMATING AND MARGIN RULES

```
Minimum GM: 30% standard. 40% if hard cost is under $2,000.
Internal labor: $150/hr — never disclosed to client.
Sub labor (Carpenter Sub #1): $100/hr → 33% GM on labor only.
  ⚠ Sub #1 fails 40% small job floor. Do not use on jobs with hard cost under $2,000.
Use "investment" framing throughout — never the word "cost" with the client.
```

---

## DOCUMENTATION REQUIRED
```
□ Client goal profile updated in portal at each annual review
□ Upgrade presentation documented — options presented, client decision recorded
□ Signed estimate on file before scope begins
□ Execution documented through S6 (Execute)
□ Home Score updated after completion (S9 — Advance the Home)
□ Invoice with margin audit on file
```

---

## COMMON MISTAKES
- Presenting upgrades before maintenance and preservation are running cleanly — wrong sequence.
- Presenting more than 2 upgrades at once — client overwhelm leads to no decision.
- Using cost language instead of investment framing.
- Skipping the three-option structure — leaves the client with nowhere to go but yes or no.

---

## HOW YOU KNOW YOU'RE DONE
Upgrade scoped, estimated, approved, executed, and documented. Home Score updated. Client understands the value delivered and its impact on their goal.

---

## RELATED SOPs
- S4 — Prioritize (upgrades surface during annual roadmap review)
- S6 — Execute (upgrade execution follows the same cycle)
- S9 — Advance the Home (Home Score updated after each upgrade)
- P4 — Estimating & Margin Audit SOP
docs.title: S8 — STRATEGIC UPGRADES SOP

docs.body:
# S9 — SCALE (ADVANCE THE HOME) SOP
## 360° Method | Phase 3: ADVANCE | Step 9 of 9

```
SOP Name:     Step 9 — Scale (Advance the Home)
360° Phase:   ADVANCE (Steps 7–9)
Work Type:    Predefined — annual review + ongoing Home Score maintenance
Who owns it:  Owner / CEO (Marcin) + Office
How often:    Full review annually. Score updated after every field visit.
Last updated: 2026-05-28
```

---

## OBJECTIVE
Measure, communicate, and advance the client's home health over time — making HP's work visible through a cumulative score, tracking trajectory toward the client's goals, and delivering the full value of the 360° Method in a way the client can see and share.

---

## WHAT IS THE HOME SCORE?
The Home Score is a 0–100 number representing overall home health across 10 system categories. Each category is scored 0–10. The total is the sum of all 10 scores.

The Home Score is HP's primary long-term value metric. It makes every visit, every project, and every preservation decision visible — showing the client that their home is improving, not just being maintained.

Updated after every field visit. Reviewed comprehensively at each annual review.

---

## SCORING SYSTEM — 10 CATEGORIES

```
Category                 Max Score   Basis for Scoring
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1.  Roof & Gutters            10      Condition rating from S2 log
2.  Exterior Envelope         10      Condition rating from S2 log
3.  Foundation & Drainage     10      Condition rating from S2 log
4.  Plumbing                  10      Condition rating from S2 log
5.  HVAC                      10      Condition rating from S2 log
6.  Electrical                10      Condition rating from S2 log
7.  Attic                     10      Condition rating from S2 log
8.  Crawl Space               10      Condition rating from S2 log
9.  Interior Systems          10      Condition rating from S2 log
10. Exterior Structures       10      Condition rating from S2 log
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
TOTAL                        100
```

**Score per category:**
```
GOOD (no issues):                 9–10
MONITOR (early-stage concern):    6–8
NEEDS ATTENTION (deteriorating):  3–5
CRITICAL (failure/hazard):        0–2
```

---

## SCORE BANDS

```
Score     Band          HP Response Posture
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
85–100    Excellent     Home is well-maintained. Focus on preservation and upgrades.
70–84     Good          Minor items in queue. Seasonal cadence holding.
55–69     Fair          Multiple SOON items. Execution cadence needs to accelerate.
40–54     Needs Work    CRITICAL or multiple NEEDS ATTENTION items. IDS trigger.
Below 40  At Risk       Significant deferred maintenance. Escalate immediately.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```

---

## WHEN TO UPDATE THE SCORE

```
Event                                        Update Required
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
After any seasonal visit (S2)               Same day — update affected categories
After any roadmap item is completed (S6)    Same day — update relevant category
After any preservation project (S7)         Same day — update relevant category
After any strategic upgrade (S8)            Same day — update relevant category
After any condition change is discovered    Same day — reflect current state
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```

---

## ANNUAL REVIEW FORMAT

The annual review is a formal meeting (in-person or video call) at each client anniversary. It is HP's most important client retention touchpoint.

```
Step 1:  Prepare the annual summary before the meeting:
           - Home Score at enrollment vs. Home Score today
           - All work completed in the past 12 months with investment totals
           - All roadmap items: completed, in progress, upcoming
           - Preservation windows entering in the next 12 months (S7)
           - Upgrade opportunities aligned with client's current goal (S8)

Step 2:  Open the meeting:
           "Here's where your home was when we started. Here's where it is today.
           Here's what we did together to get here."
           [Show score progression chart in portal]

Step 3:  Walk through the next 12 months:
           - Confirm seasonal visit calendar (S5 — Schedule)
           - Present 1–2 preservation or upgrade items for the coming year (S7, S8)
           - Review budget expectations

Step 4:  Confirm Proactive Path renewal:
           - Confirm subscription tier is still the right fit
           - Present any tier upgrade if the client's home and goals have grown

Step 5:  Ask for the referral:
           "Is there anyone in your network — neighbors, family, colleagues — who
           would benefit from having a system like this for their home?"
           Log any referrals in portal immediately.

Step 6:  Document the full review in portal:
           - Updated Home Score
           - Next year's confirmed seasonal dates
           - Upcoming project priorities
           - Referrals given (if any)
```

---

## RESALE / REFINANCE PREPARATION

For clients approaching a sale or refinance, the Home Score and portal documentation package is a value-delivery asset:

```
HP can provide:
  - Complete condition history from baseline through current date
  - All work completed with dates, photos, and invoice references
  - Current Home Score with system-by-system breakdown
  - Pending roadmap items (disclosed proactively — builds trust with buyers)

How to position:
  "HP-managed homes come with documentation. Buyers and appraisers can see
  exactly what was maintained, when, and by whom. That's a differentiated asset."
```

---

## DOCUMENTATION REQUIRED
```
□ Home Score updated after every visit and project completion — same day
□ Annual review conducted at every client anniversary
□ Annual summary document in portal: score history, work completed, next year plan
□ Confirmed seasonal dates for the coming year logged
□ Referrals logged immediately
□ Any Proactive Path renewal or tier change documented
```

---

## COMMON MISTAKES
- Not updating the Home Score after a completed project — clients can't see progress if the score doesn't move.
- Skipping the annual review — this is the retention meeting. Missing it = churn risk.
- Not asking for the referral — every annual review is a referral opportunity.
- Presenting the score without context — always show the trajectory, not just the number.

---

## HOW YOU KNOW YOU'RE DONE
Home Score is current. Annual review is complete and documented. Next year's schedule is confirmed. Client has been asked for a referral. Portal reflects the full history of HP's work on the home.

---

## RELATED SOPs
- S2 — Seasonal Inspection Log (primary data source for score updates)
- S4 — Prioritize (annual review feeds next year's roadmap)
- S5 — Schedule (annual review confirms next year's calendar)
- S7 — Preservation Projects (identified at annual review)
- S8 — Strategic Upgrades (presented at annual review)
- P10 — Referral Program SOP (referral ask happens at annual review)
docs.title: S9 — SCALE (ADVANCE THE HOME) SOP

docs.body:
# HANDY PIONEERS — SOP LIBRARY INDEX
## Pioneers Compass | Phase 2, Step 4: Write the Playbook

> The 360° Method is a proactive home maintenance framework.
> Handy Pioneers delivers that framework. These SOPs define exactly how.
> Every step has a documented SOP. Every person executing that step
> follows the same protocol. Consistency is the product.

---

## THE 360° METHOD — CORRECTED 9-STEP STRUCTURE

```
PHASE 1 — AWARE            PHASE 2 — ACT              PHASE 3 — ADVANCE
Know the Home              Take Action                 Build Value
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━   ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━   ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Step 1: Baseline           Step 4: Prioritize          Step 7: Preserve
        Assessment                 (urgency + budget           (preservation
                                    + goals + cadence)          projects)
Step 2: Inspect            Step 5: Schedule
        (seasonal log)             (seasonal cadence   Step 8: Upgrade
                                    + one-off jobs)            (strategic)
Step 3: Track              Step 6: Execute
        (client portal)                                Step 9: Scale
                                                               (Home Score +
                                                                value review)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━   ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━   ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```

> Steps 2-6 are the RECURRING CYCLE — runs 4x per year per client.
> Step 1 is the one-time foundation. Steps 7-9 activate at 12+ months.

---

## BUILD STATUS KEY
- **Active** — Written, tested, in use
- **Draft** — Written, not yet field-tested
- **Build** — Not yet written
- **Pending** — Dependent on another SOP first

---

## THE NON-NEGOTIABLE FIRST THREE
*Required before enrolling any Proactive Path client.*

```
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
#   SOP Name                          File                     Status
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
S1  Step 1 — Baseline Assessment      SOPs/S1-Baseline.md      DRAFT
    The foundation. Every client relationship starts here.
    Owner-led always. Must be in place before client #1.

S4  Step 4 — Prioritize               SOPs/S4-Prioritize.md    DRAFT
    The close. Turns findings into an agreed plan.
    The roadmap is what the client is buying.

S5  Step 5 — Schedule                 SOPs/S5-Schedule.md      DRAFT
    The recurring promise. Sets the seasonal calendar
    with the client. Without this, the PP has no cadence.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```

---

## PHASE 1 — AWARE SOPs (Steps 1-3)

```
SOP LIBRARY — PHASE 1: AWARE
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
#   SOP Name                          Owner          File                   Status
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
S1  Step 1 — Baseline Assessment      Owner/CEO      SOPs/S1-Baseline.md    DRAFT
    One-time. Full home walkthrough. 10 systems rated and
    photographed. Foundational record for all future visits.
    Non-delegated — Marcin leads every baseline.

S2  Step 2 — Inspect (Seasonal Log)   Lead Tech      SOPs/S2-SeasonalLog.md DRAFT
    Recurring. Runs 4x per year. Answers: what changed
    since last visit? Measures against baseline.
    NOT the old Condition Log — that was a one-time rubric.
    S2 is the ongoing monitoring loop.

S3  Step 3 — Tracking (Client Portal) Lead+Office    SOPs/S3-Tracking.md    DRAFT
    Post-visit documentation standard. Every visit, every
    project, every condition change logged same day.
    The portal is the product clients see. No exceptions.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```

---

## PHASE 2 — ACT SOPs (Steps 4-6)

```
SOP LIBRARY — PHASE 2: ACT
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
#   SOP Name                          Owner          File                  Status
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
S4  Step 4 — Prioritize               Owner+Office   SOPs/S4-Prioritize.md DRAFT
    Holistic: urgency + budget + client goals + seasonal
    timing + one-off requests. Produces the agreed Priority
    Roadmap. Runs after baseline, reviewed after each S2 log.

S5  Step 5 — Schedule                 Office         SOPs/S5-Schedule.md   DRAFT
    Sets calendar with client: 4 seasonal visit dates
    annually + project scheduling for all approved work.
    No project work scheduled without a signed estimate.

S6  Step 6 — Execute                  Lead+Office    SOPs/S6-Execute.md    DRAFT
    Scope → Estimate → Approve → Execute → Document.
    Margin floors enforced. Written scope before every job.
    Signed estimate required. Margin audit before invoice.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```

---

## PHASE 3 — ADVANCE SOPs (Steps 7-9)

*Build when first clients approach Year 2 of the program.*

```
SOP LIBRARY — PHASE 3: ADVANCE
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
#   SOP Name                          Owner         File                   Status
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
S7  Step 7 — Preservation Projects    Owner+Lead    SOPs/S7-Preserve.md    DRAFT
    Proactive life-extension before failure. Triggered when
    systems enter preservation window. Phase 3 (12+ months).

S8  Step 8 — Strategic Upgrades       Owner+Lead    SOPs/S8-Upgrades.md    DRAFT
    ROI-driven improvements aligned with client goal:
    Sell / Refinance / Stay. Three-option structure.
    Max 2 upgrades simultaneously. Space 90+ days apart.

S9  Step 9 — Scale (Advance Home)     Office+Owner  SOPs/S9-AdvanceHome.md DRAFT
    Home Score (0-100) across 10 systems. Annual review
    at client anniversary. Score progression shown to client.
    Referral ask built in. Resale/refinance docs available.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```

---

## SUPPORTING PROCESSES (16 Total)

```
SUPPORTING PROCESSES — OPERATIONAL BACKBONE
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
#   Process                           Owner   File                       Status
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
P1  Proactive Path Enrollment SOP     Marcin  SOPs/P1-PPEnrollment.md    DRAFT
    Two entry points: Project Path conversion + direct PP inquiry.
    Qualifying criteria, tier recommendation, founding member pricing,
    8-step enrollment sequence through signed agreement → baseline scheduled.
P2  Client Onboarding Workflow        Office  SOPs/P2-Onboarding.md      DRAFT
    30-day sequence: portal setup → baseline → prioritization → seasonal dates set.
    Welcome email template included.
P3  Proactive Path Agreement Template Marcin  SOPs/P3-Agreement.md       DRAFT
    Client-facing contract. All 3 tiers. Founding member rate field.
    NOTE: Have reviewed by WA attorney before use with paying clients.
P4  Estimating & Margin Audit SOP     Office  SOPs/P4-Estimating.md      DRAFT
    30% standard / 40% small job (<$2K hard cost). Full markup tier table.
    Sub #1 (Carpenter) $100/hr = 33% GM — fails small job floor.
P5  Invoice & Collections SOP         Office  SOPs/P5-Invoice.md         DRAFT
    3 invoice types. Collections protocol through 30-day escalation.
    Sub payment: Net-7 after client payment only.
P6  Job Costing Worksheet             Marcin  SOPs/P6-JobCosting.md      DRAFT
    Estimated vs. actual cost per job. Monthly roll-up. IDS triggers.
P7  Sub Agreement Template            Marcin  SOPs/P7-SubAgreement.md    DRAFT
    Full 1099 sub contract + onboarding checklist. Current roster status noted.
    NOTE: Have reviewed by WA attorney before use.
P8  Sub Performance Scorecard         Lead    SOPs/P8-SubScore.md        DRAFT
    Job log per sub. 2 callbacks or 1 no-show = automatic review. Quarterly audit.
P9  Seasonal Visit Checklist (4x)     Lead    SOPs/P9-Checklists.md      DRAFT
    Spring / Summer / Fall / Winter — standalone PNW field sheets.
    Full task list per season with Clark County-specific flags.
P10 360 Referral Program SOP          Marcin  SOPs/P10-Referral.md       DRAFT
    Ask language, timing, follow-through protocol, referral tracking.
    Referral ask built into S9 Annual Review.
P11 Review Request Workflow           Office  SOPs/P11-Reviews.md        DRAFT
    Ask timing, text/email templates, response protocol.
    Target: 50 reviews at 4.9 stars by Dec 31.
P12 Hiring & Onboarding SOP           Marcin  SOPs/P12-Hiring.md         DRAFT
    Lead Tech profile, hiring process, 30-day onboarding, 90-day GWC check.
    Trigger: delivery demand — not a calendar date.
P13 Seat Descriptions (all 5 seats)   Marcin  SOPs/P13-Seats.md          DRAFT
    All 5 seats: Owner/CEO, Lead Tech, Office/Estimator, Marketing Lead, Finance.
    Ownership, metrics, right/wrong fit for each seat.
P14 Accountability Chart              Marcin  Pioneers-Compass/          EXISTS
    (in HP-BOS-Filled.md Section 3.1)
P15 Licensing & Insurance Checklist   Marcin  SOPs/P15-Licensing.md      EXISTS
P16 Monthly P&L Review Template       Marcin  SOPs/P16-PLReview.md       DRAFT
    Full P&L template with PP metrics, job metrics, IDS triggers, quarterly trend view.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```

---

## RECOMMENDED BUILD ORDER

```
Priority  SOP                              Why First
━━━━━━━━  ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1         S1 — Baseline Assessment         Entry point. Before client #1.       DONE
2         S4 — Prioritize                  The close. The roadmap is the value.  DONE
3         S5 — Schedule                    Sets the recurring calendar promise.  DONE
4         S6 — Execute + P4 Estimating     Every item needs margin-checked scope. DONE
5         S2 — Inspect + P9 Checklists     Needed before first seasonal visit.  DONE
6         S3 — Tracking (Portal)           Documentation standard, runs parallel. DONE
7         P1 — PP Enrollment SOP           Cannot scale without it.              DONE
8         P7 — Sub Agreement + P8 Score    Formalize Sub #1 + Plumber.           BUILD
9         P5 — Invoice & Collections       Cash flow from job one.               BUILD
10        P12 — Hiring + P13 Seats         Required before Lead Tech posting.    BUILD
11        S7, S8 — Phase 3 SOPs            Build when clients hit 12 months.     DONE
12        S9 — Scale (Advance Home)        Clients with 3+ visits.               DONE
13        P10 Referral + P11 Reviews       Build alongside S9.                   BUILD
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```

---

## SOP TEMPLATE

Copy for every new SOP. Save with file name from index above.

```
STANDARD OPERATING PROCEDURE (SOP) — HANDY PIONEERS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
SOP Name:        [Step # — Name]
360 Phase:       AWARE (1-3) / ACT (4-6) / ADVANCE (7-9) / Support
Work Type:       Predefined / Unplanned / Defining
Who owns it:     [Seat name — not a person's name]
How often:       Per visit / Per job / Per enrollment / Weekly / Monthly
Last updated:    [Date]

OBJECTIVE:
[One sentence: what does successful completion look like?]

WHAT YOU NEED BEFORE STARTING:
[Tools, documents, logins, materials, portal access required]

STEPS:
Step 1: [Action]
Step 2: [Action]
Step 3: [Action]

DECISION POINTS:
If [this happens] → do [this]

HP MARGIN CHECK (for any SOP involving pricing or job execution):
[ ] Hard cost over $2,000?  → Standard minimum: 30% gross margin
[ ] Hard cost under $2,000? → Small job minimum: 40% gross margin
[ ] Never expose cost math, markup multipliers, or internal rates to client
[ ] Sub #1 (Carpenter) at $100/hr = 33% GM → fails small job floor
[ ] Margin audit before invoicing — every job

DOCUMENTATION REQUIRED:
[ ] [Photo / log / portal update / client sign-off required?]

COMMON MISTAKES:
- [What typically goes wrong]

HOW YOU KNOW YOU'RE DONE:
[Clear signal that this SOP is complete]
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```

---

*Last updated: 2026-05-28 | Handy Pioneers LLC | Pioneers Compass v2.0*
*9 Step SOPs + 16 Supporting Processes = 25 total documents*
*S1–S9 + P1–P13 + P16: ALL DRAFT — written, field-testing begins with first PP client*
*P14 (Accountability Chart) + P15 (Licensing): EXISTS*
*Complete SOP library: 9 Step SOPs + 14 Supporting Processes written = 23 documents*

Roadmap follow-up cadence (Lead Nurturer)
docs.body:
# Roadmap follow-up cadence

This SOP is a descriptor: the work is done by the Lead Nurturer worker, not
the dispatcher. It is listed here so the SOP library shows the complete
picture of what the AI does on autopilot.

## What it does

When a homeowner submits the roadmap generator on handypioneers.com, the
Lead Nurturer schedules a follow-up drip (email/SMS drafts) according to the
`roadmap_followup` playbook stored in the `nurturer_playbooks` table. Drafts
are generated by `server/lib/leadNurturer/draftGenerator.ts`, surfaced in the
approvals inbox, and sent on approval (pre-approved copy may auto-send per
the Communication Engine rules).

## Where to change it

- Cadence steps, timing, and voice rules: the playbook editor in the admin
  UI (DB-backed, applies on next draft generation — no redeploy).
- Generation logic: `server/lib/leadNurturer/`.
- The 5-minute worker gate: `NURTURER_ENABLED` (defaults on).

docs.title: Enrollment follow-through
docs.body:
# Enrollment follow-through

A homeowner just got a portal account. Your job: if they are a new 360
membership enrollment whose baseline scan is not yet scheduled, draft ONE
short, warm welcome email that confirms the enrollment and tells them
Handy Pioneers will reach out within two business days to schedule their
baseline home scan. Otherwise, do nothing.

## Trigger payload

`payload` carries: `portalCustomerId`, `customerId` (HP customer id, may be
null), `email`, `name`, `phone`.

## Steps

1. If `customerId` is null, stop — reply with "No HP customer linked; no
   action." and make no tool calls.
2. Call `customers.get` with the customer id. Look for an active 360
   membership on the record.
3. If there is no membership, stop with "Not a 360 enrollment; no action."
4. If there is a membership, draft the welcome email with `comms.draftEmail`:
   - Subject: "Welcome to your 360 membership"
   - Warm, plain language, 3 short paragraphs max. Thank them by first name,
     confirm the membership is active, and say we'll call within two business
     days to schedule the baseline scan.
   - Tone: a thoughtful local company, not a SaaS product. No exclamation
     marks, no emojis, no hype.

## Hard rules

- Never mention pricing, costs, discounts, or internal tier names
  (bronze/silver/gold). Customers know tiers as Essential, Full Coverage,
  and Maximum — but you don't need to name the tier at all.
- Never mention subcontractors.
- One email per enrollment. If anything is ambiguous, stop and explain
  instead of guessing. The draft always goes to a human for approval before
  it is sent.

docs.title: Daily operations brief
docs.body:
# Daily operations brief

Every morning at 7:00 Pacific, assemble a short brief of what deserves
attention today. This run is read-only: gather data with the tools, then
write the brief as your final text answer. The brief shows up in the agent
activity feed; it is internal-only and never goes to a customer.

## What to gather

1. `opportunities.list` — open leads, estimates awaiting a reply, and active
   jobs. Note anything that has sat untouched for more than 3 days.
2. `invoices.query` — outstanding balances and anything overdue.
3. `kpis.get` — current numbers if available.

## The brief format

Five short sections, plain prose, no tables:

- **Needs a reply** — proposals or leads a homeowner is waiting on.
- **Money** — outstanding and overdue totals, one line.
- **Today's work** — scheduled jobs/visits if visible in the data.
- **Stuck** — anything untouched 3+ days, oldest first, max 3 items.
- **One suggestion** — the single highest-leverage action for today.

Keep the whole brief under 200 words. If a tool fails, say what's missing
and brief on what you have. Do not call any tool that drafts or sends
anything — this is an observation run only.
